entry.title: SAP Codejam on Event Driven Build Process
entry.content:
I recently participated in an SAP Code Jam focused on building event-driven processes using SAP Integration Suite (Advanced Event Mesh) and SAP Build Process Automation. The Session was ably conducted and managed by @ajay_soreng  and @Rekha_DR .

Codejams are my favorite community events. It pure code with access to the best of SAP in terms of tools, license and expertise.  In this session we had a hands-on and walkthrough of a realistic end-to-end scenario rather than a theoretical demo.

The core use case was triggered by a Business Partner creation event in a simulated SAP S/4HANA Cloud system. Once the Business Partner was created and tagged, an event was published to SAP Advanced Event Mesh, where I configured topics, queues, and subscriptions to reliably route the event.

As you see in the below soln. diagram, an event is triggered by App. The advanced event mesh is configured to handle these events and appropriately route it to the build process and finally allow user to act on it. 

Event Triggered from a Mock UI: 

Configure the Broker Manager - The best part of this code jam:

The plethora of options of Event mesh were made available to us. The Queues, RDPs, Connectors, Tyr me's etc. was a learning experience.  

As for the use case, 

using a REST Delivery Point, the event was forwarded from Advanced Event Mesh to SAP Build Process Automation, which automatically triggered a business process. From there, the flow combined event-driven automation with human interaction—a user approval step in the SAP Build Inbox—followed by an asynchronous call to a simulated third-party badge system.

The process paused, waited for a callback, and then resumed to complete the flow with a final notification and badge ID.

 

 

What stood out most was seeing how cleanly systems were decoupled. The event producer had no awareness of the consumers, and SAP Build acted purely as an orchestrator reacting to events. Configuring and testing this live made the benefits of event-driven architecture very tangible.

Overall, I enjoyed the Code Jam as it was a great practical deep dive into how SAP Advanced Event Mesh and SAP Build Process Automation.

Thanks to all the developer advocates, especially @ajmaradiaga for compiling the content on integration suite and adv. event mesh.

Until the next code jam - Ciao !

entry.title: Build custom Joule Agent to analyze blocked freight order in S/4HANA TM
entry.content:
In this blog, the objective is to build a custom Joule agent with multiple skills that analyzes a blocked freight order in the S/4HANA Transportation Management (TM) solution.

To keep the scenario simple, the agent analyzes blocks caused by time conflicts and explains them in simple business language.

What you will learn

By the end of this blog, you will understand:

  • The prerequisites for building this use case
  • How to expose TM documents as OData services consumable by Joule
  • How to create Build Actions, Joule Skills, and a Joule Agent
  • How to write basic instructions for the Joule Agent
  • How to test and fine-tune the agent

All of this can be done with minimal or no coding. With the right access and setup, even a business user can follow these steps and build a basic scenario in about 30 minutes. The approach is similar across S/4HANA solutions, with different OData services used to fetch backend data.

Let’s look at the high-level steps

  1. Pre-requisites
  2. Identify available CDS views in S/4HANA TM and wrap it with an OData service
  3. Configure SAP Cloud Connector – Joule (from public internet) needs secure communication channel to connect your ERP environment
  4. Define Destinations on your BTP subaccount - connect to the OData service via cloud connector
  5. Define Actions in Joule Studio
  6. Define Joule Skills
  7. Define Joule Agent
  8. Test!

Let’s look at the steps in detail.

  1. STEP 1: Pre-requisites

            You need the following to run this scenario,

  • An S/4HANA private or on-premise environment
  • Eclipse IDE with ABAP Development Toolkit (ADT) – see the tutorial if you haven’t done
  • SAP Cloud Connector installed in your landscape – follow this if haven’t done
  • An SAP BTP subaccount with Joule Studio (Joule studio is one of the components of SAP Build) – follow SAP Discovery Center Mission for steps to do that.

STEP 2: Identify available CDS views in S/4HANA TM and wrap it with an OData service

            Look at the SAP documentation for all available standard S/4HANA CDS views. For S/4HANA TM, see here. In this use case, we require data from Freight Order, its item and stop nodes. So, let’s login to S/4HANA system and create an OData service for the three CDS views.

Select a package and create a service definition

 

Select referenced object as the respective CDS view and create and activate the service definition for the three CDS views.

 

Once created the definition, create a service binding by right click on each definition. Follow the wizard and after creation, activate the binding and then publish it. You can see the service URL and note that down. You can as well test the service now.

 

STEP 3: Configure Cloud Connector

Login to your SAP Cloud connector and Configure cloud to On-premise for the subaccount you are going to use for Joule. ‘/’ expose all which is not ideal, but fine for testing environments.

 

STEP 4: Define Destinations on your BTP subaccount

Now login to your BTP sub-account and navigate to ConnectivityDestinations and create a destination of type ‘HTTP,’ proxytype ‘OnPremise’.

The URL must be constructed by combining virtual host URL in cloud connector with service end point. Also add additional properties as shown in the screenshot.

 

STEP 5: Define Build Actions

Now navigate to your Joule Studio (SAP Build) environment.

Navigate to the ConnectorsActions and create an action to fetch freight orders which are blocked.

Once you click create, select OData Destinations as API source. It will display all OData destinations defined in the subaccount. Pick the destination created in step 4.

 

It displays the available services in the underlying CDS view. Select Next and provide a meaningful name and description. Then navigate to the created action project.

 

Initially, the OData service will be with its basic definition. We need to add filter to fetch only filter blocked freight order (just pick execution blocked).

For this, click $filter parameter and then click Value F4 help in the popup. You can use condition editor or Text Expression.

 

Reduce output fields

In the Output tab:

  1. Select the Body parameter.
  2. Remove unnecessary fields.

For this scenario, keep only:

  • Order number
  • Description
  • UUID

You can test the action under the Test tab by selecting the destination and clicking Test.

If destination is not visible, Go to Control Tower view of Build and select Destinations and add the destination you created before.

Do these steps to create three actions to fetch root, item and stop information. Idea here is, first fetch root and then PRD item. From item node, get the FU and go to FU stop to get acceptable and planned dates. Provide all this information to Joule for analysis.

While creating action for item,

  • add a filter to restrict only PRD items.
  • Reduce the Output to have only freightUnitUUID field.

While creating action for stop,

  • Reduce output to pick only location, stoprole, stopcategory,acceptable start and end time and planned time.

STEP 6: Define Joule Skill

Navigate to Build Lobby and click create and select Joule Agent and skill. Once the project is created, click create and select Joule Skill. Provide a clear name and description of the skill.

Click on conversation starter and provide the prompt. Example ‘get execution blocked freight order with stop details and analyze the reason for block’

 

You don’t need any other input from user as we already did filter in our action to pick execution blocked freight orders.

Add the action by clicking the ‘+’ button and select our action to fetch freight order root.

 

Navigate to skill parameter (select anywhere in the editor and click >> button to view skill parameter). Select the output for http 200 code as shown below.

 

Finally bind the skill output to the trigger end of action.

 

Likewise create other skills to fetch item and stop nodes.

For item skill,

  • Skill input must be TransportationOrderUUID
  • Map skill input to action input as shown below

 

  • Finally map skill output to trigger end.

For stop skill,

  • Skill input must be FreightUnitUUID
  • Map skill input to action inputs shown below

  •  

STEP 7 – Create Joule Agent

In the project, create ‘Joule Agent’ and navigate to agent definition screen.

Define ‘Expertise’ which provides the persona of the agent and ‘Instructions’ as system prompt on how to analyze reason for blocked freight order. Write that in plain, simple English. An example as shown below.

 

Provide some few-shot learnings to the agent as part of instructions. Example below

##Example 1 (case with date 'TranspOrdStopPlanTranspDteTme' is before date 'TranspOrdStopAccptblStrtDteTme')

Given the following Freight unit stop details:

- Transportation stop acceptable start date: 10-January-2026

- Transportation stop acceptable end date: 12-January-2026

- Transportation stop planned date: 8-January-2026

// Business Rule: date 'TranspOrdStopPlanTranspDteTme' must be after date 'TranspOrdStopAccptblStrtDteTme'

// Analysis: 8-January-2026 is NOT after 10-January-2026

// Expected blocked freight order Analysis result: Since planned date is not after acceptable start dates, freight order is blocked

Select your desired LLM provider, base and advanced model. Example OpenAI, GPT-4o Mini and GPT-4o. Save the agent definition.

Navigate to the Tools tab and add the three skills to the Agent you created before,

 

At the end, your project will have one Joule Agent and three Joule Skills.

 

STEP 8: Testing!

Click on the Test icon at the top of the project. Select the actual destinations within BTP to be used at runtime.

The conversation starter will appear and click that.

 

The agent will:

  • Select the required skills
  • Execute them in the appropriate sequence
  • Map parameters between skills
  • Evaluate the data
  • Return a business-friendly analysis using the selected LLM

 

 

Summary:

This example demonstrates how easy it is to build custom Joule agents and skills.
The same approach can be applied to other S/4HANA solutions and a wide range of use cases.

This blog should help anyone new to Joule get started and build a simple proof of concept.

entry.title: Leveraging SAP Build Process Automation for Intelligent Invoice Extraction from Outlook
entry.content:
Imagine a world where your inbox doesn’t just store invoices, but actively processes them. No more manual downloads, no more mind-numbing data entry, and zero "human-error" bottlenecks.By integrating the "digital eyes" of SAP Document Information Extraction  with the orchestration power of SAP Build, organizations can now transform unstructured email attachments into clean, high-fidelity structured data in seconds. This blog explores the seamless end-to-end journey of an invoice—starting from the moment it hits an Outlook inbox, through its automated recognition and validation phase, to its final state of readiness for your core business systems.

Phase 1: Environment Readiness

The Local Bridge: The Desktop Agent 3.0 must be installed and registered to the tenant. This acts as the execution engine, enabling the cloud-based SAP Build environment to interact directly with the local Outlook application and file system.

-To set up the agent, follow this: Install and Set Up Desktop Agent 3 | SAP Tutorials

Phase 2: Agent Configuration: Creation, Assignment, and Project Binding

Once the agent is installed, it must be assigned to an environment within the SAP Build Lobby to authorize automation execution.

  • Create the Environment: Navigate to Control Tower > Environments and select Create. Provide a name, Identifier and click Create.

  • Register the Agent: Open the newly created environment and select Agent Management > Add Agent.

  • Define Agent Attributes: Navigate to Control Tower > Agent Attributes and select Create. Provide a name and choose the Type:

                        Free: For dynamic parameterization of values.

                        Predefined: For assigning specific, fixed values.

  • Assign Attribute to Agent: Under Control Tower > Agents, locate your agent and ensure it is connected. Select Manage Attributes, choose your newly created attribute, enter the corresponding value, and confirm.

  • Bind Attribute to Project: In your project’s Process Builder, navigate to Settings > Attributes. Add the same attribute and value, then save. This creates the final link, ensuring the project triggers specifically on your configured agent.

Phase 3: Building the Automation Logic

Now that the technical "handshake" is configured, we can begin building the automation. This phase focuses on creating a reliable bridge between your email inbox and your local storage.

Initializing the Automation

Start by creating a new automation in the Process Builder. This will be the engine that handles your file and email operations. Ensure you have the Outlook SDK and File System SDK added as dependencies in your project settings.

The following is the flow for extracting info from invoices, which includes opening Outlook, searching for specific emails, saving attachments, and processing the data:

Phase  4:Preparing the Workspace: Local and Outlook Setup

For the automation to successfully process documents, you must establish a specific "landing zone" for your files. This requires the following manual configurations:

1.Local File System: Manually create a folder on your computer at the path C:/temp/attachments. This folder acts as the dedicated directory where the bot will download and store email attachments before they are uploaded to the cloud or processed by the extraction logic.

2.Outlook Folder Configuration: Inside your Outlook application, you must create a new folder specifically named Invoices.

  • Setup for Testing: To test the flow, move an email containing an invoice attachment into this Invoices folder.
  • The Unread Status: Ensure the email is marked as Unread. The automation is designed to search for unread items to prevent processing the same invoice multiple times.

Phase 5: Configuring BTP Destinations

Destinations are secure connection points that allow your automation to communicate with external services. This is a two-step process: first creating them in the BTP Cockpit and then configuring them within SAP Build.

Creation in SAP BTP Cockpit

In your BTP Subaccount, navigate to Connectivity > Destinations and create the following:

1.Service & Extraction Destination: Create a destination to handle both internal routing for the Process Automation engine and the connection to the Document Information Extraction (DOX) service.

2.SAP Document Store Destination: Create a mandatory destination for  sap_process_automation_document_store. This allows the bot to move files from your local C:/temp/attachments folder into the secure SAP Document Management Service (DMS) for centralized cloud archiving.

Configuration under SAP Build

After creating them in BTP, you must explicitly link them to your project to establish the necessary communication channels:

Mapping: In your project settings, go to the Destinations tab. Map your BTP destinations to the corresponding project requirements. This step ensures that the automation knows exactly which BTP service to call for extraction and storage.

Phase 6: Deployment and Execution

With the logic built, folders prepared, and destinations mapped, the system is now a cohesive unit. The final step is to move the project from the design stage to active execution.

1.Releasing and Deploying

In the SAP Build Lobby, you must Release your project to create a version snapshot. Once released, navigate to the Deploy button. This sends the instructions to the cloud environment, where it waits for your local agent to "pick up" the task.

2.Monitoring

Once the automation completes, navigate to the Monitoring tab in SAP Build. Here, you can view the execution logs for your job.

grossAmount: The bot has correctly identified the total (e.g., $93.50).

filePath: The bot confirms where it stored the document locally (e.g., C:\temp\invoice-a (1).pdf).

recipient: It captures the specific email address the invoice was sent to.

3.The Final Flow in Action

The following is the automated flow created for extracting info from invoices:

Outlook Monitoring: The bot identifies an unread email in your manually created Invoices folder.

Local Storage: It downloads the attachment directly into C:/temp/attachments.

Data Extraction: Once the file is stored locally, the bot uses the extraction activity to communicate with the cloud. At this stage, we can successfully obtain the extracted info from the invoice.

Conclusion:

By linking Outlook to SAP Document Information Extraction through SAP Build, you transform the inbox from a storage bin into a high-speed data engine. This setup doesn't just save time—it eliminates manual errors and ensures your business systems receive accurate data instantly. It’s a simple yet powerful blueprint for turning administrative friction into a seamless, automated advantage.

Disclaimer:
All the views and opinions in this blog are my own and are made in my personal capacity. SAP shall not be responsible or liable for any of the contents published in this blog.

entry.title: SAP Build Process Automation(SBPA):S4HANA Asset Management-Automating Major Maintenance Work Request
entry.content:
This blog provides a broad outline of how SAP Business Process Automation can help maintenance teams streamline the "Major Maintenance Work" Request Form, including the Sanction Committee Governance approval process.

What is SAP Build Process Automation?

SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) is a low-code, no-code service on the SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) that enables users to automate, manage, and optimize business processes and workflows without writing code.

We can start the business process in four ways: using a request form or an API or an Event or a Scheduled Trigger.

 

My Business Use Case:

My maintenance team is preparing for a major equipment overhaul. They must present the business case with the supporting evidence to the Sanction Committee Governance for FY26 budget approval. Once the sanction committee approves the budget, the maintenance team initiates the maintenance order process.

In this blog post, I will outline the process of creating the major maintenance work request form and explain how the approval/rejection workflow operates. (Note: The automatic creation of maintenance order at the end of the approval process will be outlined in my next blog post).

For my business use case in this blog post, the process is triggered when a form is submitted. The process then continues with a combination of steps, such as Approval Forms, Email notifications until it reaches its end.

 

If you want to gain insights into how SAP JOULE skill works, please check out my latest blog posts below.

  • SAP JOULE & ME : My First JOULE Skill in SAP EHSM-Incident Management Without Coding 
  • SAP Joule Skill for Technical Completion of Maintenance Orders in S/4HANA Asset Management 

 

We will first create a business process project using SAP Build Lobby. Then, we will design the business process. Once the business process is completed, we will create and configure forms. After that, we will create and configure approval form, and finally, we will configure the email notification.

Once we complete the setup, the final result looks like this.

Let us first create a project using SAP Build Process Automation.

Business process projects are a collection of skills in SAP Build Process Automation. Projects are part of the internal business processes of a company and are defined based on business scenarios. A project can contain a set of processes, forms, automations and decisions, which are reusable artifacts.

 

Inside a project, we can create a process. We can create a business process version using a combination of artifacts (such as forms and decisions) and process controls (such as branches, conditions, and Mail Notifications).

Let us now add forms to the business process. For Instance let us use "Form" to trigger the business process.

Let us design the form using "Open Editor".

for instance, let us add Input fields with labels.

  • General Information: we can add the dropdown values wherever required, so that the maintenance team can select the desired value while submitting the form.

  • Scope Of Work

  • Cost Estimate : Projected Cost for Materials and Services

Now the trigger form is ready. Let us design the approval process form.

The approval form is used to get faster and easier approvals from the business users. These forms are converted into tasks in an automated workflow which will appear in the My Inbox of the user. We can design and modify without coding.

Let us design the Approval Form in the form builder by dragging and dropping fields into the form editor and configure the field settings. As the L1 Approver does not have to modify the data, let us make the fields "Read Only" for the L1 approver and add a new field to input L1 Approver Comments.

 

Now, let us configure the L1 Approval form and map the fields. We can use bind list wherever required. The subject that we mention in general section will be displayed when this task appears in "My Inbox". And this task will be appeared in the "My Inbox" of the provided "Recipient".

Similarly, let us create an L2 Approver Form. Let us use the option "Duplicate".

Duplicate feature is used to copy artifacts within the business process. The copy feature is used to copy artifacts across the business process. 

Similarly, let us configure the general information and map the input fields.

We have just completed the approval form for the major maintenance work request process.

My organization demands an email notification to be sent to the requestor whenever there is an action, i.e., Approve or Rejection happens on the Major Maintenance Work Request Form.

Let us configure the Header and Subject of the E-Mail for the "Rejection" process.

Now, let us configure the E-Mail Body.

Similarly, let us configure the Header, Subject and Body of the E-Mail Notification for the "Approval" process.

Once the Major Maintenance Work Request Form with two levels of Approvals  is completed, we can release and deploy it in the respective environment.

Now, Let us run the business process...

Business Case Details:

Scope Of Work Details:

Cost Estimate Details:

Now let us submit the form. 

The L1 Approver can now check "My Inbox"  to review the Major Maintenance Work Request Form.

Since the L1 Approver Comments are mandatory for this form, the option "Approve" or "Rejection" will be highlighted once L1 Approver provides the comments.

Once the Major Maintenance Request Form is approved by the L1 Approver, It will be displayed in L2 Approver`s Inbox. Let us approve it and send an email notification to the requestor upon the approval. L1 Approver comments are displayed while L2 Approver approves or rejects the form.

Once the workflow is ended, we can monitor the processes.

The monitoring page collects the events coming from the deployed workflows and automation jobs and present them to the user. These events represents the status of the jobs that have been run, and the business activity of the monitoring events.

For my business use case, we can view the process, workflow status using "Process and Workflow Instances" application.

😊This brings me to the end of process walkthrough on SAP Build Process Automation using forms as a trigger for one of the use cases, i.e., Major Maintenance Work Request.

In Summary,  we have designed and configured a digital form to capture structured data for a "Major Maintenance Work". Implemented multi level approvals (L1- L2). And also triggered automated email notifications to the requestor, improving transparency and reducing manual efforts. This entire approach established a foundation that can be scaled up and showed how automation can reduce repetitive tasks and speeds up the approval cycle with more transparency.

Stay tuned for more updates on SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Joule Skills/Agents in S/4HANA Asset Management Space...

-- 

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. C

entry.title: Joule Studio Enablement Guide
entry.content:
📑 Table of Contents

  1. Overview
  2. Use Case & Mission Description
  3. Prerequisites
  4. Setting Up SAP Build for Joule Studio
  5. Required Subscriptions & Instances in BTP
  6. Mandatory Roles & Role Collections
  7. Ensuring Required Subscriptions Are Available
  8. Adding Missing Cloud Identity Service (Additional Tenant)
  9. Formation Creation (Mandatory for Joule Integration)
  10. Enablement Complete

 

1. Overview

Joule Studio is SAP’s platform to build Joule Agents, Joule Skills, and integrate enterprise AI capabilities directly into SAP applications. It is part of SAP Build, enabling teams to create and extend business applications and AI solutions with built-in governance and clean core alignment.

 

2. Use Case & Mission Description

  • Joule Studio is activated
  • Required BTP services are set up
  • Identity and trust between Joule and IAS are configured
  • Administrators can deploy Joule agents
  • Developers can create Joule Skills, Document Grounding, and integrate custom APIs

3. Prerequisites

To activate Joule Studio, the following must be fulfilled:

Required Entitlements

  • SAP Build Process Automation — Subscriptions(build_default)
  • Joule — Subscriptions(standard)
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services — Subscriptions(additional-tenant)

Cloud Identity Services (Mandatory)

IAS is required for:

  • Authentication
  • SSO
  • Authorization management
  • Trust configuration for Joule

If you do not have IAS, you must create a new tenant.

 

4. Setting Up SAP Build for Joule Studio

Standalone activation of:

  • Joule
  • SAP Build Process Automation

 

SAP BTP Boosters/Subscriptions

Boosters automate service activation.
Navigate:

Global Account → Boosters or Create Subscriptions of each of the plans given.

Use the following:

  • “Set Up SAP Build Process Automation”
  • “Set Up Joule”

Each booster/Subscriptions:

  • Assigns roles
  • Creates role collections
  • Subscribes necessary services

 

5. Required Subscriptions & Instances in BTP

Mandatory

Service

Plan

Required For

SAP Build Process Automation

build_default

Joule Studio backend

Joule

standard

Joule Studio AI

SAP Cloud Identity Services

Additional-Tenant

Authentication, SSO

 

6. Mandatory Roles & Role Collections

The Joule Booster does NOT create a role collection.

You MUST manually create one:

Create Role Collection

  1. Subaccount → Security → Role Collections
  2. Click Create
  3. Name it: Joule_Role_Collection

Add Roles

Navigate to:
Security → Role Collections → Joule_Role_Collection → Edit

Add:

  • extensibility_developer
  • end_user

Save.

Assign to Your User

Security → Users
Assign: Joule_Role_Collection

 

7. Ensure All Required Subscriptions Are Visible in the Systems

Under:
Global Account → System Landscape → Systems

Confirm:

  • Joule (standard)
  • SAP Build Process Automation (build_default)
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services (Additional Tenant)*

(*) If missing, add manually (next section).

 

8. Adding Cloud Identity Service (Additional Tenant)

If IAS additional tenant is not visible:

Navigate:

Global Account → System Landscape → Systems → Service Owner View

Click Add System

Enter:

  • System Type: SAP Cloud Identity Services
  • System Name: any name
  • CLD Tenant ID: IAS tenant ID

Finding CLD Tenant ID

Use Cloud Reporting:

🔹 Live Landscape
https://reporting.ondemand.com/sap/crp/cdo?query=xxx&type=crp_search

🔹 Canary Landscape
https://spc-vlab.ondemand.com/sap/crp/cdo?query=xxx&type=crp_search

Replace xxx with your IAS tenant ID.

⏳Note: It may take up to 24 hours for reporting to sync newly created IAS tenants.

 

9. Formation Creation (Mandatory for Joule Integration)

Navigate:
Subaccount → System Landscape → Formations → Create Formation

1. General Information

  • Formation Name: Your Choice
  • Formation Type: Integration with Joule

2. Include Systems

Add:

  • SAP Build Process Automation
  • Joule
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services

3. Integration Details

Check the box:
Enable Capability Deployment

4. Create

System may take time (~10–30 minutes) to reach Ready status.

This deployment links Joule, BPA, and IAS together.

 

 

10. Enablement Complete — You Can Build Joule Agents

Once the Formation is Ready:

You can now:

  • Build Joule Agents
  • Build Joule Skills
  • Use Document Grounding
  • Call Custom APIs

entry.title: The Hidden Cost of Custom Code in SAP IS U — And How BTP Reduces It Without a Big Bang
entry.content:
Executive Summary

For years, SAP IS‑U has quietly carried the weight of utility operations. Billing accuracy, regulatory compliance, and meter‑to‑cash stability always came first — and rightly so. But the side effect of that stability is a landscape full of Z‑programs, user exits, and enhancements that were built for good reasons at the time, yet now create friction when utilities try to modernize.

As the industry shifts toward decarbonization, DER growth, and real‑time customer expectations, the question isn’t “Is custom code a problem?” It’s “How do we reduce its impact without breaking the systems that keep the lights on?”

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) offers a practical, low‑risk way to separate custom logic from the core and prepare for S/4HANA — without a disruptive rewrite or a multi‑year freeze.

1. The Custom Code Reality Inside SAP IS‑U

 Most custom code in IS‑U wasn’t a mistake. It was a necessity.

Utilities added enhancements to:

  • Meet state‑specific regulatory rules
  • Support complex rate structures
  • Integrate with legacy CIS, AMI, and market systems
  • Automate workflows long before APIs or event frameworks existed

Over time, this created:

  • Thousands of Z‑objects
  • Enhancements buried deep inside billing, device management, and FICA
  • Business logic tightly coupled to SAP’s upgrade cycle

What once delivered flexibility now slows down change.

2. The Hidden Costs Most Programs Underestimate

2.1 Upgrade and Migration Friction

Every enhancement inside the core increases:

  • SP and EHP regression testing effort
  • S/4HANA conversion complexity
  • Dependency on niche IS‑U technical expertise

Custom code does not just slow upgrades — it dictates upgrade timing.

2.2 Operational Risk

Many utilities still rely on:

  • Poorly documented enhancements
  • Knowledge concentrated in a few long‑tenured developers
  • Custom logic triggered deep inside critical transactions

This creates silent operational risk — especially during incident response or regulatory audits.

2.3 Innovation Tax

Each new digital initiative must answer an uncomfortable question:

“Do we modify the core again, or build around it?”

When the core is overloaded with custom logic, innovation slows — not because ideas are weak, but because change becomes expensive and risky.

3. Why ‘Rewrite Everything’ Is Not a Strategy

Most utilities cannot absorb:

  • A full custom code rewrite
  • A large‑scale CIS replacement
  • A multi‑year freeze on business change

A big‑bang approach often introduces more risk than it removes.

A more realistic path is selective decoupling — and that’s where BTP fits naturally.

4. How SAP BTP Reduces Custom Code Impact — Pragmatically

BTP gives utilities a way to shift custom logic out of the core while keeping IS‑U stable.

4.1 Keep the Core Clean — for Real

Instead of embedding logic in IS‑U:

  • Business rules move to BTP services
  • Extensions run side‑by‑side, not inside ECC or S/4HANA
  • Core processes remain upgrade‑friendly

4.2 Event‑Driven Extensions

Using events and APIs:

  • IS‑U triggers events (billing completion, device updates, move‑in/out)
  • BTP processes logic asynchronously
  • Failures are isolated, not system‑wide

4.3 Reusable Innovation Layer

Once on BTP, the same extension framework can support:

  • DER orchestration
  • AI‑based billing analytics
  • Customer self‑service enhancements
  • Regulatory reporting automation

This turns custom logic into assets, not liabilities.

5. Reference Architecture (Conceptual)

Below is a simplified conceptual architecture illustrating how SAP IS‑U and BTP coexist.

This model allows utilities to:

  • Retire high‑risk custom code gradually
  • Introduce new capabilities without touching the core
  • Prepare for S/4HANA on their own timeline

6. A Phased, Utility‑Friendly Adoption Model

Phase 1: Observe

  • Identify custom code hotspots
  • Monitor usage, performance, and change frequency

Phase 2: Isolate

  • Move non‑core logic to BTP extensions
  • Replace enhancements with APIs and events

Phase 3: Optimize

  • Retire unused Z‑objects
  • Standardize extension patterns
  • Accelerate future innovation

This approach delivers value within existing budgets and without destabilizing operations. 

7. Final Thought for Utility Leaders

The goal is not to eliminate custom code overnight. The goal is to stop letting yesterday’s decisions dictate tomorrow’s options.

SAP BTP provides a practical, low‑risk path to reduce technical debt, lower long‑term cost, and modernize SAP IS‑U — without a big bang and without compromising the core.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture, not migration dates.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture — not migration dates.

entry.title: Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean Core, BTP Driven Strategy for ModernUtilities
entry.content:
Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean‑Core, BTP‑Driven Strategy for Modern Utilities

Executive Summary

If you’ve spent any time around utility billing operations, you know mass reversals aren’t rare — they’re part of the job. Sometimes it’s a rate issue, sometimes a meter read problem, and sometimes it’s just one of those unexpected data surprises that ripple across months of billing, these reversals often span multiple billing cycles and thousands of customers. While the operational process may take days to complete, the customer impact is immediate: as soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on their online portals. This triggers confusion, social media chatter, call‑center spikes, and reputational risk before corrected bills are ready.

What I want to share here is a practical way utilities can handle this without rewriting IS‑U or scrambling teams at 6 AM. We used SAP BTP to create a small visibility layer that sits between IS‑U and the customer portal — and honestly, it solved a problem that has bothered billing teams for years, event‑driven architecture, and lightweight AI to prevent premature credit visibility without modifying SAP IS‑U or customer‑facing systems. The solution introduces a “Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer” that controls what customers see during mass reversals, ensuring operational accuracy without customer panic.

The Practitioner Reality: When a Simple Reversal Becomes a Public Crisis

Every utility billing team has lived through this scenario:

  • A mass correction requires reversing 8–12 months of invoices.
  • The reversal job runs for hours or days.
  • As soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on the portal.
  • Screenshots appear on social media: “Utility X just gave me $900 credit!”
  • Call centers are overwhelmed.
  • Regulators ask questions.
  • The billing team is still in the middle of the correction.

The root cause is simple: SAP IS‑U posts reversals immediately, and customer portals read them immediately. And here’s the part nobody talks about: IS‑U has not built‑in buffer. The moment a reversal posts, the customer sees it. There’s no ‘hold this until we’re done’ button. That’s where the trouble starts. Utilities cannot legally notify customers in advance, and they cannot delay the operational correction. This creates a structural gap between internal billing processes and external customer visibility.

Why This Happens: A Technical Gap in Traditional IS‑U

SAP IS‑U was designed with a real‑time posting model:

  • Reversal document posts →
  • FICA updates →
  • Portal reads →
  • Customer sees credit

There is no concept of:

  • “Temporary suppression”
  • “Billing in progress”
  • “Visibility hold”
  • “Batch publish”

This is not a failure of IS‑U — it’s simply a design that predates modern digital customer expectations.

The Modern Solution: A Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP

Instead of modifying IS‑U or customer portals, utilities can introduce a lightweight, clean‑core extension on SAP BTP that controls visibility, not billing.

This layer sits between:

SAP IS‑U → BTP → Customer Portal / CIS / Mobile App

It does not change billing logic. It does not alter FICA. It does not modify the portal. It simply orchestrates when billing information becomes visible.

Key Capabilities

  • Detect mass reversals in real time
  • Flag accounts as “under correction”
  • Temporarily suppress credit visibility
  • Replace credit display with a neutral message
  • Release final billing once corrections are complete

This simple layer kept customers from seeing half‑baked credits and gave the billing team the breathing room they needed to finish the job properly.

Textual Representation

 

How It Works Using BTP Services

1. SAP Event Mesh — Detect Reversals in Real Time

Event Mesh listens for:

  • Billing document reversals
  • FKK document reversals
  • Mass job triggers

When reversal volume exceeds a threshold (e.g., >500 accounts), it triggers the suppression workflow.

2. SAP BTP Workflow — Manage the Correction Window

Workflow orchestrates:

  • Start of reversal window
  • Suppression period
  • Completion of rebilling
  • Release of final invoice

This creates a controlled, auditable process.

3. SAP BTP Business Rules — Define Visibility Logic

Rules can be configured without coding:

  • “If reversal count > X, suppress visibility for Y hours.”
  • “If account is in correction, show neutral message.”
  • “If rebill fails, extend suppression.”

This keeps the solution clean‑core and upgrade‑safe.

4. SAP HANA Cloud — Temporary Data Store

Stores:

  • Accounts under correction
  • Suppression flags
  • Workflow status
  • Rebill completion indicators

No changes to IS‑U tables.

5. SAP BTP AI — Predict and Prevent Customer Impact

AI can:

  • Identify accounts likely to call
  • Detect social media spikes
  • Predict rebill duration
  • Recommend suppression windows
  • Flag anomalies in reversal patterns

This turns a reactive process into a proactive one.

What the Customer Sees Instead

Instead of a large credit, the portal displays:

“Your billing is being updated. Your final balance will be available shortly.”

This avoids:

  • Panic
  • Misinterpretation
  • Social media escalation
  • Call center overload

Clean‑Core Alignment

This approach is fully clean‑core because:

  • No IS‑U modifications
  • No FICA changes
  • No portal rewrites
  • No custom Z‑tables in SAP
  • All logic runs on BTP
  • Fully upgrade‑safe
  • Works with ECC and S/4HANA

This is exactly what SAP means by “extensibility without modification.”

Practitioner Example

In one of the utilities I worked with, we had to reverse nearly 10 months of bills for over 180,000 customers. Within 45 minutes, screenshots of huge credits were all over social media. The call center was drowning. Regulators were pinging leadership. It was chaos — and all because the portal updated too early.

After implementing a BTP‑based visibility layer:

  • Mass reversals ran silently
  • No customer saw temporary credits
  • No social media chatter
  • No call center spike
  • Corrected bills were published cleanly

The billing team completed the same process with zero customer disruption.

Conclusion

Mass invoice reversals will always be part of utility operations. But customer visibility does not need to be immediate or uncontrolled. By introducing a Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP — powered by Event Mesh, Workflow, Business Rules, HANA Cloud, and AI — utilities can protect customer experience, reduce operational risk, and maintain clean‑core principles.

This is modernization where it matters: not in rewriting billing logic, but in orchestrating how and when customers see the results.

entry.title: Joule Studio: Documentation for Document Grounding and Testing, Deploying & Launch Joule Agent
entry.content:
Note: Enablement of Joule Studio (IMPORTANT): Joule Studio Enablement Guide

📑 Table of Contents:

  1. Introduction
  2. What Is Document Grounding in Joule Studio?
  3. Architecture Overview
  4. Prerequisites
      SAP Services Requirements
      Access Requirements
      Required Credentials from Service Keys
  5. Setting Up Destinations
      AI Core Destination
      Object Store Destination
  6. Configuring Document Grounding in Control Tower
      Create Resource Group
  7. Monitoring Status
  8. AWS S3 CLI Guide for Uploading Documents for Grounding
  9. Viewing Grounding Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad
  10. Adding Document Grounding to Joule Studio Agents
  11. Using Joule Agent - Test in Private Environment and Deploy & Launch Joule Agent
  12. Troubleshooting

1. Introduction

Document Grounding is a capability in Joule Studio that lets you attach custom documents (PDFs, manuals, SOPs, policies, knowledge bases) to an AI agent.
This ensures the agent gives highly accurate, contextually relevant, and source-backed answers.

In older setups, admins had to manually manage:

  • Generic Secrets
  • Base64 encoding
  • Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad

Now, Joule Studio automates these tasks through Control Tower, simplifying the job for developers.

 

2. What Is Document Grounding in Joule Studio?

Document Grounding performs these functions:

  1. Reads documents stored in an S3-compatible Object Store
  2. Creates a grounding pipeline inside SAP AI Core
  3. Creates a Resource Group to store the pipeline + embeddings
  4. Makes the resource available to Joule Studio Agents

This allows the agent to:

  • Retrieve answers strictly from the grounded documents
  • Reduce hallucinations
  • Use enterprise-verified content

3. Architecture Overview

Key interactions:

  • Control Tower creates Resource Groups
  • AI Core runs embedding pipelines
  • Object Store provides documents
  • Joule Studio binds the grounding data to agents

 

4. Prerequisites

You must have:

SAP Services

  • SAP AI Core (INSTANCE - Extended Plan)
  • SAP AI Launchpad (APPLICATION - Standard Plan)
  • Object Store (INSTANCE - s3-standard or standard)
  • SAP Build Control Tower Access 
  • SAP Build Lobby with Joule Studio enabled (Joule Studio Enablement Guide)
  • Add all the roles to the user from BTP Cockpit → Security → User → (select the user(s)) → Role Collections → Assign Role Collections → Select all the roles related to that (SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, Object Store, and SAP Build )

NOTE: For Object Store plan Standard, make 2 keys with Role "Read" and "Write".

 



Access Requirements 

  • Admin role for Control Tower
  • Developer role for Joule Studio
  • Object Store admin or contributor
  • AI Core admin or contributor

Required Credentials from Service Keys

  • AI Core Service Key
  • Object Store Service Key

5. Setting Up Destinations

Two destinations must be configured:
1. AI Core Destination
2. Object Store Destination
These allow Joule Studio to perform grounding operations behind the scenes.

AI Core Destination

Step 1 — Retrieve AI Core Service Key

From BTP → Instance → Service Key

Important fields:

  • url
  • clientid
  • clientsecret
  • tokenurl (must modify)

Step 2 — Configure Destination

Destination Type: HTTP

Field

Value

URL

<AI Core Service Key URL>

Client ID

clientid

Client Secret

clientsecret

Token Service URL

<AI Core URL>/oauth/token (must append /oauth/token)

Proxy Type

Internet

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Step 3 — Add Additional Properties

These ensure visibility in Joule Studio.

Property

Value

sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-ai-core

 true

Important Note

If you forget to append /oauth/token, the grounding pipeline will not trigger.

 

Object Store Destination

Step 1 — Retrieve Object Store Service Key


Note: For Standard Plan, Use the Read role key for the destination.

Extract values such as:

  • access_key_id
  • secret_access_key
  • host
  • bucket
  • region

Step 2 — Configure Destination

Destination Field

Service Key Field

URL

host (add https://)

Client ID

access_key_id

Client Secret

secret_access_key

Token Service URL

host (add https://)

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Proxy Type

Internet

Step 3 — Additional Properties

Property

Value

region

region from service key

bucket

bucket value

username

username from service key

type

S3

sap-joule-studio-document-grounding-object-store

true

These properties ensure that Joule Studio can detect the Object Store.

 

 

6. Configuring Document Grounding in Control Tower

Control Tower now includes a Joule module with Document Grounding options.

Admins decide:

  • Which Object Store to use
  • Which AI Core instance will create pipelines

6.1 Create Resource Group

Steps:

  1. Go to SAP Build Control Tower
  2. Navigate:
    Control Tower → Joule → Document Grounding
  3. Click Add
  4. Provide a Resource Group Name
  5. Select the AI Core Destination
  6. Select the Object Store Destination
  7. Click Create

A new resource group is created and grounding begins automatically.

 

7. Monitoring Status

Resource group states explained:

Status

Meaning

Pending

Pipeline running; embeddings not finished

Completed

Successfully grounded

Error

Incorrect destination or corrupted document

Troubleshooting:

  • Check destination credentials
  • Ensure documents are not corrupted
  • Ensure Object Store bucket name matches

Note:

After approximately few minute (depending on the size of the documents), the status will change to Completed.

In earlier instances, error messages occasionally appeared, or the status remained Pending for an extended period. At times, the status alternated between these messages. In such cases, it is important to ensure that the destinations are configured correctly and that the documents in the Object Store are not corrupted.

 

8. AWS S3 CLI Guide for Uploading Documents for Grounding

1. Install AWS CLI

Run the following command:

brew install awscli

2. Configure AWS CLI

After installation, open Command Prompt (Windows) or Terminal (Mac/Linux) and configure the AWS CLI using your AWS credentials.


Note: For Standard Plan, Use the Write role key for the uploading the documents.

Run the following command:

aws configure

You will be prompted to enter:

  • AWS Access Key ID
  • AWS Secret Access Key
  • Default region name (for example: eu-central-1)
  • Default output format (for example: json, or press Enter to leave it as default)

3. Upload a Grounding Document to AWS S3

To upload a local file to an S3 bucket, use the following command:

aws s3 cp <local-file-path> s3://<bucket-name>/

Example:

aws s3 cp user/…/LA.pdf s3://my-grounding-bucket/

4. Verify File Upload

To confirm that the file was uploaded successfully, list the contents of the S3 bucket:

Note: For Standard plan user again need to configure AWS CLI with read key or you can use Cyberduck for checking the uploaded docs and AWS CLI for uploading.

aws s3 ls s3://<bucket-name>/

If the file appears in the list, the upload was successful.

9. Viewing Grounding Pipelines in SAP AI Launchpad

Once grounding is triggered:

  1. Go to SAP AI Launchpad
  2. Add API Connection
  3. Once loaded, go to: Workspaces → Select the resource Group

    Note: Wait for few minutes until, the resource group is created.


  4. Navigate:
    AI Core Administration → Resource Groups
  5. You will see a new group named:
    {ResourceGroupName}{UnixTimestamp}

  6. Select/Click the resource group, the go to
    Generative AI Hub → Grounding Management → Click on pipeline created.
    This confirms grounding is complete.

    Note: If the pipeline is showing "This data repository is currently empty", then check the destination and s3 bucket.

 

10. Adding Document Grounding to Joule Studio Agents

Developers now consume the resource group.

Steps:

  1. Project Creation: Open Joule Studio → Lobby → Create button → Joule Agent and Skill / Joule Skill
    Give Project Name and Description. Click on the project

  2. Create Joule Agent: Create → Joule Agent

  3. Fill all the necessary details carefully and meaningfully. 
  4. Navigate to Tools Section → Add Tools → Documents


  5. Provide:
    • Tool Name
    • Description
  6. Select one of the available Admin-created Resource Groups

The agent now has grounding enabled.

 

11. Using Joule Studio - Test in Private Environment and Deploy & Launch Joule Agent

  1. Save the Agent.
  2. Make sure Private Environment for testing must be activated: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (Environments) → Click on Private Environment Active.
  3. Enable Generative AI: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (Generative AI) → Enable Generative AI.
  4. Enable Generative AI: Control Tower → Tenant Configuration (AI Agent Builder Activation) → Enable Agent Builder.
  5. Joule Agent Window → Test → Select Private → Continue.


  6. Test is woking


  7. Release the Joule Agent


  8. Make a Environment for deploying the Agent: Control Tower → Environments → Create 


  9. Deploy to created environment.


     

  10. Launch: Control Tower → Environments → Select the created environment agent_test_env → More (Joule) → Launch 


    You can use the link as per your preference.


12. Troubleshooting

Common issues:
- Wrong destination credentials
- Missing '/oauth/token'
- Corrupted PDF files in Object Store
- Resource group stuck in Pending

entry.title: Setup Actions and Events for integration in SAP Build Process Automation for SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
This blog explains how to do the setup for Actions for a Sales Order scenario and how to consume this in SAP Build Process Automation. It also has reference for business events setup.

 

Event Setup

If you are using event-based integration for starting the process using Event Triggers please refer to the links for the setup S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition ( Configure SAP Enterprise Messaging for SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2020 via Service Key ) and S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition ( Event Mesh and SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Setup for SAP Build Process Automation).  Here is a detailed link describing how to use Event Triggers in SAP Build Process Automation Discovering Business Events from Lobby for SAP Build Process Automation : Manage Credit Memo Request

 

Actions Project Setup

“Actions” in SAP Build Process Automation connects processes to external systems, whether SAP or non-SAP. This is crucial for automating or expanding business processes for various LoB processes such as S/4HANA, Ariba, and SuccessFactors. With SAP Build Process Automation, these extensions can be easily built, and through actions, connections can be made to S/4HANA, Ariba, or other SAP LoB systems for GET, POST, PATCH, and other types of calls.

In this blog we will create an action project based on Sales Order API with the following steps.

  • Step 1 - Set Up Connection to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition
  • Step 2 - Set up Communication Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Edition
  • Step 3 - Add destination in SAP Build
  • Step 4 - Create Action Project in SAP Build
  • Step 5 - Test the Action Project
  • Step 6 - Release and Publish the Action Project.

Step 1 – Set Up Connection to SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition

1)  Enable APIs in SAP Gateway Client

Please follow this blog post to enable the API for the service API_SALES_ORDER_SRV.

2)    Configure Cloud Connector

Please follow this blog post steps to configure the cloud connector.

  • Configure Destination for SAP Build Process Automation
  • After the Cloud Connector is configured, you should be able to see the Cloud Connector in your SAP BTP Subaccount under Connectivity -> Cloud Connectors.
  • Now let’s create destination for Sales Order API from BTP Cockpit->  Subaccount -> Destinations

  • Make sure to provide the following additional properties while creating the destination:

sap.applicationdevelopment.actions.enabled = true

sap.processautomation.enabled = true

sap-client=<ClientID>

  • Click on Check Connection and test that the connection is successful.

Step 2: Set up Communication Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 

Prerequisite: please make sure you should have an administrator access for SAP S/4HANA system.

For the following setup, we will use communication scenario for Sales Order Integration (SAP_COM_0109).

Optional: You can learn more about communication scenarios here.

  1. Create Communication User

1.1 Login to SAP S/4HANA Cloud System and select 'Communication Management' group:

1.2 Select ‘Maintain Communication Users’ App and click 'New':

1.3 Afterwards, go ahead and provide User Name, Description & Password and click 'Create':

1.4 Once you click 'Create', the Password Status becomes Productive. Now click 'Save': 

Great! The Communication User is now successfully created.

Next up, Communication System and Communication Arrangement need to be created.

2. Create Communication System:

2.1 Login to SAP S/4HANA Cloud System, select 'Communication Management' group and Click 'Communication Systems' app (similar to step 1.1).

2.2 Here you would see the list of already existing Communication Systems if any. Click 'New' to create a new Communication System:

2.3 Enter a System ID and System Name and click 'Create':

2.4 Provide the Host Name and HTTPS Port of the system you want to connect to.

We will use a hypothetical name and port here.

Click 'Add' button for inbound communication to add the user for authenticating inbound request:

Note: Here we are using inbound service hence the 'Logical System' is optional but when we make use of outbound service then 'Logical System' plays an important role and is mandatory to be filled.

2.5 Select the Communication User (from value help) which we had just created. The assigned Communication User will now automatically appear in Username field. Click 'Save'.

2.6 Click 'Save' and the Communication System is created:

Now we have successfully created a Communication System with a Communication User assigned to it.

3. Create Communication arrangement based on the relevant Communication Scenario:

3.1 Login to SAP S/4HANA Cloud System, select 'Communication Management' group and Click ‘Communication Arrangement’ app (like step 2.1).

3.2 Once you are within the 'Communication Arrangements' application you would see the existing Communication Arrangements if any. Click 'New':

3.3 Here select the relevant 'Communication Scenario' (from value help) based on the API you would want to access. Like in our example we will use Sales Order Integration (SAP_COM_0109) for this case and click 'Create':

 

Note: You would be able find relevant Communication Scenarios in the API documentation from SAP API Business Hub.

3.4 Select the 'Communication System' (from value help) which we have just created. The assigned Communication User will now automatically appear in User Name field. Click 'Save':

The Communication Arrangement is now created successfully.

4. Configure Destination for SAP Build Process Automation

We will now create the destination for Sales Order APIs. 

First, go to SAP BTP Cockpit -> Subaccount -> Destinations. You will see the available destination listed. Make sure that the additional properties are as below:

sap.applicationdevelopment.actions.enabled = true

sap.processautomation.enabled = true

sap-client=<ClientID>

In this example, we are using the destination DCOM_Sales_Order as a reference only.

And finally, click on Check Connection to test it.

The connection should indicate OK:

Step 3: Add destination in SAP Build

Next, to use this destination, we will connect it to SAP Build Process Automation. From the SAP Build lobby, click on Control Tower.

Click on Destinations, to configure the destination with SAP Build:

Look for the Destination name from the previous step in the search bar and click on the 3 dots: 

Afterwards, we specify the Environment in which the destination will be configured to. Choose any environment  (here the screenshot shows Public) and Add Destination.

Environments refer to the different stages or platforms where the automated processes will run, such as development, testing, and production. Each environment may have different sets of configurations and requirements.

 

 

Step 4: Create Action Project in SAP Build

  1. Please download the pre-configured Action Project by clicking here. You will download the mtar. file from GitHub to your device as below:

2. Now head on over to the SAP Build Lobby. Click on the Import function:

3. Browse the imported file from your machine to import it. 

 

4. Great, now that you have imported the Action project. Let's review it. Go to Actions function on the left-hand side. Then, double click on the Sales Order project.

5. This will open the Actions Editor function in a new tab with the Action project downloaded. As you can see there are 3 APIs inside:

Step 5 - Test the Action Project

It is now time to test the APIs. Let's start testing starting with the GET API: Reads the header of a sales order. Click on it:

 

After clicking on the configured destination, click on the Test tab and provide a valid SalesOrder number. Click on the Test button:

 

Test response reading the header of the relevant Sales Order will be provided as the following:

 

Now, from the Actions tab, and let's test the other GET API: Reads all items of a Sales Order by following the same steps as before:

 

Check Response:

 

Great job! We have now configured and tested the actions you downloaded successfully for our exercise.

Step 6 - Release and Publish the Action Project

 

You will now release the action project to create version(s) and then publish that version in the action repository. It is then these published actions that can be used in different processes and applications to connect to external systems.

To release a version of the action project, click Release from top-right corner.

 

In the release popup, enter the Release Notes of your choice and click Release.

If you have Publish to Library button instead of Release, please directly Publish as prescribed in the next step.

 

Once the action project is released, you can then publish the action by clicking Publish from top-right corner and confirm Publish. Wait for confirmation that it is published.

 

With this you have successfully completed creating, configuring, releasing and publishing of action project.

Now you can use these published actions in any SAP Build Process Automation project.

entry.title: SAP BPA Visibility Scenarios Explained: Monitoring and Optimize Business Processes
entry.content:
Introduction: Submitted a workflow in SAP Build Process Automation and then wondered, "Where did it go? Is it stuck? Did someone approve it yet?"
Yeah, we've all been there! That's exactly where Visibility Scenarios come to the rescue. Think of them as your center for monitoring all your business processes in real-time.

What you'll learn today:
  • How to create visibility scenarios that actually matter
  • Monitor your workflows 
  • Track down specific instances 
  • Get insights that help you optimize your processes

I have explained the visibility scenario and how to implement it in BPA in this blog.

Note: To create a Visibility Scenario, your application must have at least one process

Using a visibility scenario, you can monitor and analyze process data through the process visibility dashboard.

Step 1: Go to your project and select 'Create'; here, you will have an option for a visibility scenario

Step 2: Name your visibility scenario 'My Visibility Scenario'

Step 3: Click add button 

Step 4: Click 'Add Process'. (More options are available here; you can select whatever is needed based on your project requirements)

Step 5: Here, you can choose the process flow that needs to be added to the visibility scenario

Step 6: Click 'Save' to save the Visibility Scenario. After saving, you need to release and deploy the project. Only then will it be visible in the Control Tower.

Step 7: Go to the Control Tower > Environment, select the environment, and click the dropdown to choose the project where we created the visibility scenario.

Step 8: In that tab, at the top right corner, you have the 'Navigate to Dashboard' button you can click that.

Step 9: Once you click the 'Navigate to Dashboard' button, you will see the following output for your visibility scenario.

Note: The Instance ID in SAP Build Process Automation (BPA) Workflow and the Instance ID shown in Process Visibility are completely independent.
This means you cannot directly search for a process in Visibility using the BPA Instance ID.

However, there is a solution.
You can expose the BPA Instance ID as a custom attribute in Process Visibility. This allows you to search and filter Visibility records using the BPA Instance ID through an F4 help (value help).

Below are the steps to achieve this:

1. Create a custom variable in your BPA workflow to store the BPA Instance ID.

2.In the workflow, assign the BPA Instance ID to the newly created custom variable.

 

3.In the Visibility Scenario, create a new attribute and map it to the custom variable.
This attribute will be displayed in Process Visibility and can be used for filtering.

4. Save all changes and deploy your project so that the visibility configuration is updated.

5. After deployment, reload the Process Visibility application.
You will now see the BPA Instance ID (exposed as your custom attribute).
You can use it to search for any specific workflow execution started by the user.

Conclusion: The SAP Visibility Scenario in BPA empowers users to gain insights into business processes. With this, you can monitor open instances, failed instances, completed instances, suspended instances, and canceled instances. For open instances, you can also view their status—whether they are critical, at risk, or on track.

entry.title: End to End guide to setup Joule, SAP Build Process Automation, and SAP Build Work Zone on SAP BTP
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Modern enterprises rely on multiple SAP and third-party applications to run their business processes. However, navigating across different systems and extracting insights quickly can be challenging for users. To address this, SAP is embedding artificial intelligence directly into its enterprise solutions through Joule, SAP’s AI copilot.

Joule transforms how users interact with SAP systems by enabling natural language interactions, automating routine tasks, and surfacing relevant business insights instantly. Combined with SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) and SAP Build Work Zone, organizations can build intelligent workflows, integrate APIs, and deliver AI-powered business experiences through a unified digital workspace.

1.1 What You Will Learn in This Blog

In this blog, we will walk through the end-to-end setup and integration of Joule with SAP Build services on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). You will learn:

  • An overview of Joule and its role as SAP’s AI copilot

  • The architecture and environment required to run Joule on SAP BTP

  • How to configure SAP Build Process Automation and create Joule skills

  • The setup steps required in SAP BTP, including Cloud Identity Services and service entitlements

  • How Joule skills interact with APIs, workflows, and destinations

  • How SAP Build Work Zone enables users to access Joule-powered experiences through a unified workspace

1.2 Joule: SAP’s AI Copilot

Joule is SAP’s AI copilot designed to simplify how users interact with enterprise applications. It provides a conversational interface that helps employees complete tasks, retrieve insights, and navigate across SAP systems more efficiently.

Designed as a companion for the Intelligent Enterprise, Joule offers role-based assistance across the SAP solution portfolio. Through natural language interactions, users can search information, access relevant applications, and perform business transactions without navigating complex menus.

By integrating directly with business applications, Joule enhances productivity, improves decision-making, and streamlines daily operations.

1.3 Environment and Architecture

Joule is built using modern cloud-native architecture on SAP BTP.

Key architectural characteristics include:

  • Runs on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
  • Built as a multi-tenant application, ensuring strict data isolation between customers
  • Secure and enterprise-ready architecture
  • Compliance with AI ethics guidelines and GDPR standards

This architecture enables organizations to safely leverage generative AI capabilities within their SAP landscapes.

1.4 Key Features

  • Deep SAP Integration
    Joule is embedded directly within SAP applications, allowing users to interact with business processes without leaving their working environment.
  • Conversational User Interface
    The assistant provides a conversational UI built with SAP Fiori-compliant controls, enabling intuitive interactions.
  • Tailored Capabilities
    Joule adapts its assistant capabilities based on the SAP solutions implemented within the organization.
  • Continuous Innovation
    New capabilities and improvements are delivered automatically as part of SAP’s cloud updates.

2. Introduction to SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA)

SAP Build Process Automation is a low-code platform on SAP BTP that enables organizations to automate business processes, integrate systems, and build intelligent workflows.

SBPA combines multiple capabilities in one solution:

  • Workflow automation
  • Business rules management
  • AI-assisted process development
  • Joule skill creation using Joule studio

Within the Joule ecosystem, SAP Build Process Automation plays a critical role by enabling developers to create skills and workflows that Joule can trigger through natural language interactions.

For example, a Joule request such as: “Create a purchase request for office supplies” can trigger an automated workflow built in SBPA that interacts with backend systems.

2.1 Introduction to Joule Studio

Joule Studio is the development environment used to create and configure Joule skills and actions.

It allows developers to:

  • Integrate APIs
  • Define conversational capabilities
  • Build workflows
  • Connect enterprise systems
  • Test AI-assisted interactions

Joule Studio works closely with SAP Build Process Automation, enabling developers to design intelligent workflows that Joule can execute when users submit requests.

2.2 Initial Setup on SAP BTP

Before configuring Joule and related services, several prerequisites must be met in the SAP BTP cockpit.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following service plans are available in your global account:

A subaccount must be created in a Joule-supported data center region.

Step 1: Configure Custom Identity Provider (Cloud Identity Services)

Check if a Cloud Identity tenant already exists. If available, note the tenant URL: https://<tenant>.accounts400.ondemand.com. If not, create a new tenant.

Assign Entitlement

Navigate to: Subaccount → Entitlements → Add Service Plan→ Cloud Identity Services→ additional-tenant plan

Create Instance

Service Marketplace → Cloud Identity Services → Create Instance

Activate Tenant 

Activate the tenant through the email sent by SAP.

Step 2: Create a User in the Subaccount

Navigate to: Security → Users → Create User

Provide:

  • Name
  • Email
  • Identity Provider (Cloud Identity tenant)

Assign the required role collections to the user.

Step 3: Configure SAP Build Process Automation

Establish Trust

Subaccount → Security → Trust Configuration → Establish Trust

Enter the Cloud Identity tenant URL.

Add Entitlement

Add the service: SAP Build Process Automation – build-default plan

Create Instance

Service Marketplace → Process Automation → Create Instance

Assign Roles

Assign the following role collections to the Cloud Identity user:

  • Process Automation Admin
  • Process Automation Developer
  • Process Automation Participant
  • Process Automation Viewer

Step 4: Configure SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)

Add Entitlement

Add the service: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition – standard plan

Create Instance

Service Marketplace → SAP Build Work Zone → Create Instance

Assign Roles

Assign role collections such as:

  • Launchpad_Admin
  • Launchpad_User

Step 5: Run the Joule Booster

Navigate to: Global Account → Boosters

Search for: Setting Up Joule and start the booster.

Select Configuration

Choose:

  • das-application – standard plan
  • Target subaccount

Select products and integrations such as:

  • SAP Build Process Automation
  • SAP Build Work Zone

Resolving Prerequisite Errors

If prerequisites are missing, ensure the Cloud Identity tenant appears in:

BTP Cockpit → Systems

If not visible:

  • Request access to the Cloud Reporting tool: https://reporting.ondemand.com/sap/crp/cdo
  • Search for your custom tenant and copy the CIS Tenant ID. It may take a day to reflect the tenant idon cloud reporting tool after the custom tenant creation
  • Add it under: Global Account → Systems → Service Owner View→ Add Cloud Identity services system→ tenant ID

Assign Joule Roles

Create a role collection: Joule Administrator

Add the following roles from the das-application service:

  • end_user
  • extensibility_developer
  • tenant_admin

Assign the role collection to your Cloud Identity user.

Final Outcome

After completing these steps:

  • Joule is provisioned in your BTP subaccount
  • Identity trust is established
  • SBPA and Build Work Zone are integrated
  • Required roles are assigned

2.3 Joule Skill Development using SAP Build Process Automation:

SAP BTP Destinations

  • This is where you register external APIs that your Joule skill will call.
  • A destination in SAP BTP acts as a connection configuration (e.g., endpoint URL, authentication type, credentials) for external systems or APIs.
  • Example: A REST API that retrieves employee details or triggers a business workflow.

Action: You first create or register the API endpoint as a destination in SAP BTP.

Environment (in SAP Build Process Automation)

  • The environment is the runtime where Joule skills are deployed and executed.
  • It is linked to the BTP destinations you registered earlier.

Action:

  • You attach the environment to your destinations.
  • This ensures your Joule skill has permission to call those APIs at runtime.
  • The environment can also be shared with SAP Build Work Zone or Joule Standalone.

Joule Action

  • This is where you define the REST API calls your Joule skill will use.
  • You configure the GET/POST/PUT/DELETE methods, define request/response payloads, and test the API output.

Action:

  • Create a Joule Action that calls the registered destination API.
  • Validate that the API response works as expected (just like postman)

Project (Joule Skill Workflow)

  • Within SAP Build Process Automation, you create a Project that designs the skill logic (workflow) for your Joule skill.
  • You integrate your Joule Action (from the previous step) inside this workflow.
  • This project defines how the skill behaves when invoked — the steps, logic, and output.

Action:

  • Design the workflow for your skill.
  • Integrate your Joule Action using REST APIs to handle inputs/outputs.

SAP Build Work Zone
SAP Build Work Zone provides a unified digital workspace where users access business applications. It enables organizations to create business sites that bring together:

  • SAP applications (such as SAP S/4HANA)
  • Custom-built applications
  • Third-party solutions

These applications can run in the cloud or on-premises, but are delivered through a single unified user experience. Once Joule skills are created and deployed, they can be integrated into SAP Build Work Zone so users can invoke them directly from the interface.

  • This is where your application is hosted.
  • It provides the front-end interface through which users interact with Joule-integrated applications.

Action:

  • Once your Joule skill is ready, it can be integrated and accessed from within the SAP Build Work Zone.
  • This integration allows users to invoke the skill directly from the UI.

Joule Standalone

Joule can also operate independently outside of SAP Build Work Zone. In standalone mode, it still uses the same SAP Build Process Automation environment and BTP destinations, allowing Joule skills and integrations to function across different interfaces.

Shared Environment

Joule Standalone and SAP Build Work Zone share the same SAP Build Process Automation environment, which contains the configured destinations and deployed workflows. This shared setup allows developers to build and manage integrations once and reuse them across multiple interfaces, ensuring consistency and easier maintenance.

Workflow Summary:

  1. Register API: Create a destination in SAP BTP for the external API.
  2. Attach Environment: Link the environment with BTP destinations.
  3. Configure Joule Actions: Define API calls (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) and validate responses.
  4. Build Project: Create workflow (skill logic) integrating the Joule Action.
  5. Deploy Environment: Joule deploys the skill here using attached destinations.
  6. Integrate UI: Expose the skill through SAP Build Work Zone or Joule standalone.

3. Introduction to SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Work Zone is a modern digital experience platform that enables organizations to create unified, role-based, and personalized access to applications, business processes, and information across both SAP and non-SAP systems.

It delivers a launchpad-style user experience, allowing users to access SAP Fiori applications, custom-built applications (such as CAP apps), third-party web applications, documents, and collaboration tools through a single unified interface.

By centralizing application access and content delivery, SAP Build Work Zone simplifies the user experience and improves productivity across the enterprise.

SAP Build Work Zone Editions

SAP Build Work Zone is available in two editions designed for different business needs.

Edition

Purpose

Standard Edition

A lightweight, launchpad-focused solution ideal for internal business applications and SAP Fiori experiences.

Advanced Edition

A comprehensive digital workplace solution built on SAP BTP and Cloud Identity Services, enabling intranet capabilities, collaboration features, and enterprise-wide content management.

Most application-centric implementations — including Joule integrations — typically start with the Standard Edition.

3.1 Core Components of SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Work Zone is composed of several key components that help organize applications, manage access, and design user experiences.

Sites

A Site represents the main digital workspace or portal where users access applications and content. It defines the overall structure and experience of the workspace, including navigation, pages, branding, and available applications.

Key characteristics:

  • Acts as the entry point for users
  • Contains pages, apps, and navigation menus
  • Supports branding and layout customization
  • Can be internal-only (Standard Edition) or public-facing (Advanced Edition)

Organizations often create different sites for departments, business units, or use cases.

Site Directory

The Site Directory is the central place where administrators manage and access all created sites.

From the Site Directory, administrators can:

  • Create new sites
  • Manage existing sites
  • Assign site administrators
  • Configure navigation and structure
  • Launch the site editor

It acts as the starting point for designing and managing digital workspaces within SAP Build Work Zone.

Content Manager

The Content Manager is the central administration tool used to manage and organize all site content within SAP Build Work Zone. It allows administrators to configure applications, pages, navigation structures, and access permissions for users.

Through the Content Manager, administrators manage several key components that define how content is presented and accessed in the workspace.

Apps

Apps represent the applications that users interact with inside SAP Build Work Zone. These applications can originate from various sources and are integrated into the workspace to provide a unified access experience.

Apps can include:

  • SAP Fiori applications
  • Custom-built applications such as CAP applications
  • External web applications
  • Embedded third-party solutions

Applications are typically integrated using:

  • SAP BTP Destinations
  • HTML5 Application Repository

This integration approach enables users to access applications seamlessly, regardless of whether they run on SAP BTP, external platforms, or on-premise systems.

Pages

Pages define the layout and structure in which applications and content are displayed within a site. Each page can contain multiple sections that organize different types of content and applications.

Common page elements include:

  • Tiles – clickable application launch icons
  • Cards – dynamic widgets that display real-time data or insights
  • Sections – logical groupings used to organize tiles and cards on a page

Pages can be created in two ways:

  • Custom-designed pages created by administrators to meet specific business needs
  • System-generated pages, such as default home pages or task-related pages like My Inbox

This flexible page structure allows organizations to design role-based and personalized digital experiences for their users.

Spaces

Spaces provide a high-level navigation structure within a site. They group related pages together and help organize the workspace for different business roles or functions.

For example, organizations might create spaces for:

  • Finance
  • Human Resources
  • Procurement
  • Analytics

Each space can contain multiple pages that organize relevant applications and content.

Catalogs

Catalogs are collections of applications that can be assigned to roles. They act as containers that group related apps together, making it easier to manage and distribute content across users.

Administrators typically organize catalogs based on business functions, departments, or solution areas.

Roles
