Roles control access to catalogs and the applications within them. When roles are assigned to users, they automatically gain access to the catalogs and apps associated with those roles.

When SAP Build Work Zone is first subscribed to a subaccount, a default role called Subaccount_Everyone is automatically created. This role includes all users within the subaccount by default. Administrators can use this role to assign common content that should be accessible to every user, such as:

  • Home pages
  • General applications
  • Company-wide tools
  • Shared informational content

Additional custom roles can be created to support department-specific or role-based access control.

Channel Manager

The Channel Manager is used to import and manage business content from external providers such as SAP systems.

It allows administrators to subscribe to content channels, which automatically bring preconfigured applications into Work Zone.

Common use cases include importing:

  • SAP S/4HANA Fiori apps
  • SAP SuccessFactors apps
  • SAP Business Technology Platform content

Once a channel is subscribed, the apps become available in the Content Manager, where they can be assigned to catalogs and roles. This significantly simplifies the process of integrating SAP application content into Work Zone.

Settings and Joule Enablement

The Settings section in SAP Build Work Zone allows administrators to configure system-level features and integrations. From the settings panel, administrators can configure:

  • Site branding and theming
  • Authentication and identity integration
  • Feature toggles and extensions
  • Joule AI integration

Enabling Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

To enable Joule within Work Zone:

  1. Navigate to Site Manager
  2. Open Settings
  3. Enable the Joule AI Copilot option
     
  4. Ensure the Joule service (das-application) is correctly configured in SAP BTP
  5. Assign required Joule roles to users
  6. Once enabled, users can interact with Joule directly within the Work Zone interface, allowing them to:
  • Search for information
  • Trigger workflows
  • Access applications through natural language
  • Retrieve insights from SAP systems

This integration brings AI-powered assistance directly into the digital workspace, further improving productivity and user experience.

entry.title: Orchestrating AI Assisted HR Workflows with Microsoft Copilot Using SAP BTP and SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Employees increasingly expect quick answers to questions such as:

  • What is the status of my promotion request

  • Which onboarding tasks are still pending for new hires

The objective was to allow AI-assisted HR queries while maintaining strict governance over SAP SuccessFactors data and workflows.

The organization had very clear requirements. AI tools should not have direct access to SuccessFactors APIs, and they should not execute actions using external tokens. All interactions had to remain fully controlled, auditable, and aligned with HR governance policies.

To achieve this, we designed an architecture where SAP BTP served as the orchestration and control layer between Microsoft Copilot and SuccessFactors.

When a user interacts with Copilot and asks a question related to HR processes, the request is not sent directly to SuccessFactors. Instead, it is routed to SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP.

At this stage, the integration layer performs several important functions. The request is validated, enriched with user context, and evaluated against access policies. This ensures that the request complies with the organization’s HR security and authorization rules.

Once validated, the request triggers a workflow through SAP Build Process Automation. This workflow layer applies business rules and approval logic related to HR processes. For example, certain HR data may only be accessible to managers or HR partners, and some actions may require additional validation steps.

Only after these governance checks are completed does the integration layer interact with SAP SuccessFactors modules such as Employee Central, onboarding workflows, or performance management services.

The requested information is retrieved in a controlled manner and transformed into a simplified response that can be returned to the AI interface.

From the user’s perspective, Copilot appears to instantly provide HR insights. However, behind the scenes the request has passed through a fully governed integration and workflow orchestration layer within SAP BTP.

This approach provided several advantages for the organization. AI could enhance the employee experience without introducing uncontrolled access to HR systems. All interactions remained traceable and compliant with internal policies. At the same time, the architecture created a scalable foundation for future AI-driven HR scenarios.

As enterprises continue integrating AI assistants into workplace tools, the key challenge will not be enabling AI itself but ensuring it operates within controlled enterprise architectures. Platforms such as SAP BTP provide the necessary capabilities to bridge modern AI services with critical business applications while maintaining governance, security, and operational visibility.

entry.title: Unlock Enterprise-Scale Integration and Automation with SAP: A 5-Part Webcast Series
entry.content:
Running integration at enterprise scale requires more than connecting systems - it demands a unified strategy, consistent governance, and a strong operational foundation. To help organizations navigate this complex landscape, we’re excited to announce a deep-dive 5-part webcast series that shows how SAP Integration Suite and SAP Business Technology Platform can serve as a true enterprise-wide integration platform.

Whether you’re looking to simplify complexity, strengthen governance, or enable future-ready automation, this series provides a structured path from vision to sustainable execution. Here’s a breakdown of what you can expect from each session:

 

1. Rethinking Integration: Building a Unified Enterprise-wide Platform

Integration starts with consolidation and unification. This session explores how organizations can position SAP Integration Suite as a central platform, connecting systems, processes, and data across the enterprise. Learn strategies to streamline your integration landscape and create a foundation for scalable, enterprise-wide operations.

Register here:  https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5256733&sessionid=1&key=2F2D713929E44EC97A0255F0674D0915&groupId=6661068&sourcepage=register

This session dives into the steps organizations can take to develop a scalable integration strategy that aligns business goals with technology execution. Discover best practices for governance, architecture, and execution that drive measurable value.

Register here: https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5256733&sessionid=1&key=2F2D713929E44EC97A0255F0674D0915&groupId=6661111&sourcepage=register

 

3. Achieve Enterprise-grade Operations for SAP Integration Suite

Operational excellence is critical to sustaining integration at scale. In this session, we focus on how to strengthen governance, monitor performance, and build resilient processes with SAP Integration Suite. Learn how enterprise-grade operations ensure consistency, reliability, and scalability across your integration landscape.

Regiser here: https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5259511&sessionid=1&key=905F0343E0BA503A283C97B3645BCB31&groupId=6667225&sourcepage=register

 

4. Drive Intelligent Business Transformation with Agentic Enterprise Automation

Intelligent business transformation requires more than isolated automation - it demands connected, adaptive processes powered by AI. Explore how SAP Business Technology Platform and SAP Business Transformation Management enable agentic enterprise automation at scale. Learn how to design, govern, and measure AI agents that drive true process transformation, generating measurable outcomes across complex enterprise environments.

Register here: https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5259684&sessionid=1&key=10935CA2537B96624DD065F7EA514C2F&groupId=6661382&sourcepage=register

 

5. Develop Robust Automations with SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite

Agentic enterprise automation only succeeds when systems, workflows, and applications are seamlessly connected. This session demonstrates how SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite work together to enable end-to-end automation across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. See live demos, customer success stories, and practical examples showing how low-code development and enterprise-grade integration accelerate automation while maintaining control, visibility, and scalability.

This series is designed for integration architects, IT leaders, and business transformation professionals who want to take their enterprise integration and automation initiatives to the next level. Join us to learn how SAP can help you simplify complexity, achieve operational excellence, and unlock intelligent, future-ready automation.

Save your spot 🙂

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - Rethinking Paper-Based Trade (Part 1 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

After participating in the SAP Multidimensional Architect Program at the SAP Academy  in California during Fall 2025, I was particularly inspired by a session titled “From Blueprint to Real Architecture,” which showed how to design end-to-end solutions, one of the cases presented, was international trade scenario, area of my personal interest over the last few years. That experience motivated me to write this Proof of Concept.

This article focuses on the scenario of Ocean Carriers transitioning from paper-based processes to a fully digital platform, aiming to reduce CO₂ emissions while ensuring security and transparency among stakeholders. The solution implements the Electronic Bill of Lading using IMDA's  TradeTrust , an open-source framework that enables the creation of digital trade documents represented as NFT(ERC-721 tokens) on a blockchain network.

This PoC demonstrates how this capability can be deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform, aligned with Digital Container Shipping Association (DCSA)  standards.

 

Article Summary

This three-part series demonstrates how international trade can move from paper-based operations to secure and automate processes while helping organizations achieve their sustainability goals

  • Part 1 – Case Study: Introduces the shift in global trade from paper-based documents to Electronic Bills of Lading.
  • Part 2 – Blueprint:  Describes the key building blocks of the solution, including: event-driven architecture, SAP BTP, Automation, TradeTrust Framework, UI, and the architecture diagram.
  • Part 3 – End-to-End Scenario: Shows the lifecycle of the Electronic Bill of Lading through a real-world scenario.

 

1. Case Study

The global shipping industry, responsible for approximately 90% of world trade, faces pressure to reduce its environmental footprint. Traditional paper-based Bills of Lading, critical for international trade, contribute to deforestation, water pollution, and greenhouse gas emissions through their production, transportation, and disposal.

2. Business Context

Paper Bills of Lading(BL) plays a crucial role in international trade, serve as Receipt, Contract and Document of title. This document is been the core of international trade for centuries.

  • Receipt: The BL, which is issued by the carrier, serves as proof that the cargo has been loaded onto the vessel, documenting the quantity and condition of the goods at the time of shipment.
  • Contract: It acts as a contract of carriage between the shipper, carrier and consignee, defining the agreed terms and conditions of transportation.
  • Document of title: It acts as a document of title, representing ownership of the goods while they are in transit. Possession of the original bill of lading allows the holder to claim the goods upon their arrival or arrange transfer ownership of the cargo to another party in the supply chain.

 

The process typically starts with the carrier issuing the BL to the shipper/seller upon receiving the goods. The shipper/seller then sends the original bill of lading to the buyer, often through a bank, to ensure that the seller receives the payment before the goods are released to the buyer. Finally, the consignee/buyer presents the original bill of lading to the carrier at the destination port to take possession of the goods.

Problem

Traditionally, this has been paper-based, the document must travel physically between banks and buyers across countries, requiring couriers, manual signatures, and physical handovers. Each party checks it manually, which takes 5-10 days, and if the cargo is traded while the ship is at sea, the bill changes hands multiple times. This often causes ships to wait at port, creates demurrage costs, and forces parties to use risky workarounds like letters of indemnity, which carry legal and financial risks.

                                                                 Total time needed for B/L transfer via express courier service: 5-10 days

Solution

The emergence of blockchain technology has enabled the development of Electronic Bills of Lading(eBL), offering not only enhanced security and efficiency but also the potential for a substantial reduction in paper consumption and associated carbon emissions.

Blockchain provides a decentralized and transparent system to manage eBL. The data is not controlled by a single party, but shared across a network where all participants can verify the transactions. Every action, such as issuing or transferring the eBL, is recorded on the blockchain and can be traced by authorized parties. Transactions are validated almost in real time, allowing the eBL to be transferred within minutes instead of several days required with paper documents sent by courier.

By eliminating millions of paper documents and the need for courier shipments, organizations can reduce delays, lower operational costs, minimize the risk of errors, and decrease carbon footprints while aligning with ESG commitments. Electronic bills of lading on an Ethereum Layer 2 can cut carbon emissions by ~96% compared to traditional paper B/Ls.

Electronic Bill of Lading Lifecycle[4]

 

The benefits of the eBL are many and huge. In the announcement, DCSA wrote: “Switching away from the transfer of physical paper bills of lading could save $6.5 billion in direct costs for stakeholders, enable $30-40 billion in annual global trade growth, transform the customer experience and improve sustainability.”

Payment Settlement in International Trade

In traditional international trade, payments are typically secured through banking instruments such as Letters of Credit, documentary collections, or bank guarantees. In these models, banks act as trusted intermediaries that verify trade documents, including the Bill of Lading, before releasing payment from the importer to the exporter. While these mechanisms provide security, they are often manual, time-consuming, and highly dependent on paper-based documentation.

As trade processes move toward digital documentation, new opportunities emerge to integrate payment mechanisms directly with Electronic Bills of Lading.

By combining digital payments with eBL, parts of the trade process could be automated. For example, a smart contract on a blockchain funded by the importer could hold the payment until predefined conditions are met. Once ownership of the eBL is transferred to the importer, the smart contract could automatically release the funds to the exporter. In this context, stablecoins may play an important role by enabling fast and programmable digital settlement.

At the same time, modern banks increasingly provide APIs for Letter of Credit verification and trade finance services. Through networks such as SWIFT, financial institutions can exchange standardized payment messages and verification data. These capabilities can be integrated into digital trade platforms, helping automate document verification.

Although payment is not explored in detail within the scope of this Proof of Concept, the architecture presented here is designed to support such capabilities in the future, allowing payment orchestration to be incorporated as the trade ecosystem continues to evolve.

The Future of Global Trade

In February 2023, DCSA announced a multi-carrier agreement to get to 100% usage of electronic Bills of Lading by 2030.

Thomas Bagge, chief executive officer, DCSA stated, “The digitalization of international trade holds vast potential for the world economy by reducing friction and, as trade brings prosperity and the eBL will further enable trade, helping bring millions out of poverty. Document digitalization has the power to transform and requires collaboration from all stakeholders.”

3. Solution Implementation

In order to develop a fully digitalized solution for international trade that leverages blockchain technology for the Electronic Bill of Lading, the solution should be aligned with industry standards to ensure interoperability, operational efficiency, and regulatory compliance. The solution follows the guidelines established by the Digital Container Shipping Association (DCSA)  and the United Nations Centre for Trade Facilitation and Electronic Business(UN/CEFACT) .

To support the implementation of the Electronic Bill of Lading, this Proof of Concept leverages TradeTrust , an open-source framework developed by the Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) . TradeTrust provides a neutral infrastructure for the digitization and secure exchange of trade documents, aiming to make international trade safer, faster, easier, and more cost-effective.

The framework uses a public blockchain to maintain a single source-of-truth record of document ownership and aligns with the principles of the MLETR(Model Law on Electronic Transferable Records) , established by the UNCITRAL(United Nations Commission on International Trade Law). 

How TradeTrust Works

Conclusion

In Part 1, I explored the benefits of the Electronic Bill of Lading and its potential to transform the industry—driving sustainability, improving transparency across the supply chain, and accelerating global trade operations.

🔗In Part 2 , I’ll walk through the technical design and core building blocks behind the solution.

entry.title: Terraform in Practice: Setting Up Joule Studio
entry.content:
Introduction 

As Developer Advocates, we run SAP CodeJam events where participants need hands-on access to the Joule Studio environment, typically provided by us during the day of the event. The challenge: a Joule Studio tenant has limits on the number of deployments that can be made, and these limits are reached in a CodeJam setting where 30+ developers are simultaneously testing their skills and agents, pushing deployments etc, so each CodeJam event requires spinning up a brand new tenant from scratch knowing which subscription, instances, plan, role collections, destinations, and credentials to use while creating a new tenant. 

Doing these tasks by hand is a long and tedious process. It takes too many clicks and careful typing, which makes it easy to make a mistake. With CodeJam events happening frequently, this process was becoming a real bottleneck. Terraform seemed like a good choice at this point to perform these steps. Instead of repeating the same manual steps every time, we could automate the entire tenant provisioning process, turning what used to take significant manual effort into a few commands and getting a Joule Studio tenant at the end of the execution which typically takes 20 to 30 mins. 

I had the opportunity to explore Terraform during a hands-on session at SAP Inside Track, Bangalore in June 2025.  After trying it out in the developer hands on area, I could see its value for automating the creation and management of SAP BTP subaccounts. During Devtoberfest 2025, SAP experts on Terraform presented a session titled From Clicks to Code: Automate SAP BTP Setup with Terraform on how to set up a BTP environment with Terraform. While trying to learn more about Terraform, I also came across a learning.sap.com course that is comphrensive. The course is available here and the GitHub repository used in this learning course is available here. There are also some excellent posts in the SAP Community portal for beginners looking to get started with Terraform, the links to those posts can be found in the Additional References section. Rather than repeating what's already well-documented, I'll focus on the key concepts behind the Terraform and share a few things I picked up along the way and provide you a Terraform script to get started with Joule Studio setup.  

What is Terraform? 

Terraform uses HashiCorp Configuration Language for building infrastructure with configuration files. Terraform's configuration language is declarative, meaning that it describes the desired end state for your infrastructure. It also keeps track of your real infrastructure in a state file, which acts as a source of truth for your environment. Terraform uses the state file to determine the changes to make to your infrastructure so that it will match your configuration. Terraform builds a resource graph to determine resource dependencies and creates or modifies non-dependent resources in parallel. This allows Terraform to provision resources efficiently. Terraform plugins called providers lets Terraform interact with cloud platforms and other services via their application programming interfaces (APIs). SAP provides dedicated Terraform providers for BTP, Cloud Identity, Cloud Foundry, and Cloud Connector. The BTP provider functions by wrapping BTP CLI commands, allowing you to convert imperative CLI commands into declarative Terraform resources. See the registry for documentation on how to use them: https://registry.terraform.io/search/providers?namespace=SAP. 

Using Terraform as an Administrator 

There are two ways to run the terraform script to create a Joule Studio environment. We will look at how to run the Terraform scripts on a local machine with Terraform CLI first. The approach mentioned below requires minor changes depending on whether we are running it on a Mac or Windows machine. 

  1. Clone this repository https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform
  2. Open it in VS Code
  3. Go through the pre-requisites section in the README file 
  4. Install Terraform CLI https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/tutorials/aws-get-started/install-cli 
  5. Set up environment variables by running these commands on your VS Code terminal  

On Mac/Linux 

export TF_VAR_btp_username='your-btp-username' 
export TF_VAR_btp_password='your-btp-user-password'

On Windows (PowerShell) 

$env:TF_VAR_btp_username = 'your-btp-username' 
$env:TF_VAR_btp_password = 'your-btp-user-password'

6. Configure the values for global account subdomain, identity provider domain, subdomain name and region in the terraform.tfvars file

Project in VS Code 7. Go to the root directory and run the commands from your terminal one by one. 

terraform init
terraform apply --auto-approve

The terraform init command installs the SAP BTP and Cloud foundry providers configured in the main.tf file in the repository.  

The terraform apply command installs the entitlements and subscriptions mentioned in the .tf files. 

8. Once all the resources are created, run Joule booster to create formation to complete the Joule studio setup. See the steps here on running the Joule booster. 

9. Navigate to the Instances and Subscriptions page of your new BTP subaccount; ensure the following subscriptions and service instances have been successfully provisioned.

The second approach is explained in the GitHub Actions section. 

GitHub Actions 

Now, the local Terraform CLI approach works perfectly fine and well suited for a development workflow, but what if you would rather not install anything on your machine at all? 

That's where GitHub Actions comes in. GitHub Actions lets you automate anything, triggered either by an event in your repository (like a push or a PR) or kicked off manually straight from the GitHub UI. Your workflows live as YAML files inside the .github/workflows directory of your repository. 

Check out the README of this repository to follow this approach for creating your Joule Studio setup: https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform 

One thing worth knowing: you can override any terraform.tfvars variables defined in your repository with the corresponding values in the GitHub variables by passing them via the --var flag. You can see how to pass the variables in the terraform.yml file under .github/workflows of the repository. Anything you haven't explicitly overridden will simply fall back to whatever is defined in terraform.tfvars. 

Use this approach when you have a stable version of a script that can be used to create a new tenant with all the resources and dependencies. 

Using Terraform as a Developer 

My recommendation would be to stick to the local Terraform CLI approach while you are actively writing or updating your scripts. The reason is simple: when something fails during development, Terraform lets you fix just the broken parts and apply only those changes. That kind of control is needed when you are iterating quickly. 

Before you start customizing the script to fit your needs, there are also a handful of expressions and constructs worth getting accustomed to.  So, let’s walk through the significant ones. 

Variables 

All the variables used in the script are defined in the variables.tf file with an optional default value if needed. Configurable values such as global account name, sub account region etc can be provided in the terraform.tfvars file. Sensitive information like passwords and secrets should be stored as environment variables in local machines or as GitHub secrets in GitHub. Terraform makes this easy with the TF_VAR_ prefix. Any environment variable with that prefix gets automatically picked up as a Terraform input variable. So, if you set TF_VAR_btp_password in your environment, Terraform maps it directly to var.btp_password in your script. This keeps credentials in GitHub Secrets or Environment variables and passes them securely to Terraform without exposing them in logs or files. 

If a variable has no default value and you haven't provided it through .tfvars or an environment variable, Terraform will prompt you for it in the terminal during plan or apply. It won't silently fail, but it will interrupt your flow, so it's worth getting your variables sorted before you run anything.  

Built -in functions: 

There is a set of built-in functions available that you can use within your scripts from this reference. Some of the functions used in the repository are lower(), replace(), trim(), toset(), contains() etc. When a subaccount is created, the name of the subaccount subdomain needs to confirm certain rules defined by BTP. We could use these built-in functions to sanitize the user input provided in the terraform.tfvars file and create a subaccount subdomain that confirms those naming conventions.  

raw_subdomain = lower("${var.subaccount_name}-${var.subaccount_subdomain}-${var.subaccount_region}") 
subaccount_subdomain = trim (replace (replace (raw_subdomain, "/[^a-z0-9-]/", "-"), "/--+/", "-"), "-")

This code sanitizes and formats a string to create a subdomain by concatenating three variables into lowercase and using regular expressions to replace any invalid special characters or double-hyphens with a single dash.

Meta-argument 

depends_on is a meta-argument to define explicit dependency. For eg: if you want to create a subscription for Build Work Zone but the entitlement for Build Work Zone should exist before the subscription happens. In such cases, the depends_on argument can be used to ensure that the entitlement is created before the subscription. By default, Terraform executes resources in parallel.

local values block  

When you want to define a local variable or derive a value from a variable, you can use locals. For instance, the cf_api_url is derived from subaccount_region variable, so we can define it within local value block and then use them by referencing it as local.cf_api.url  

locals {
  cf_api_url = https://api.cf.${var.subaccount_region}.hana.ondemand.com 
}

Ternary operators 

Ternary operators can be used to apply conditional expressions. 

plan_name = var.environment == "prod" ? "standard" : "free"

If the environment is production, it assigns the value "standard" to plan_name.

What I learned about the Development workflow 

 After working with Terraform, here is what I consider to be the best practices to follow: 

  • Always start with terraform fmt -recursive before committing anything. It ensures your code style remains consistent and identifies basic syntax issues early, preventing them from causing problems later
  • Run terraform validate after every code change. It's quick, doesn't need credentials, and gives you instant feedback on syntax and data type errors 
  • Never skip the terraform plan -out=tfplan even for "small" changes. This step shows you exactly what Terraform is about to create, modify, or destroy. Review the output carefully before moving on
  • Then apply with terraform apply tfplan using the saved plan file. This guarantees you are applying exactly what you reviewed 
  • Avoid --auto-approve flags in production
  • Never commit .tfstate files as state files can contain sensitive data like passwords and secrets. Add this file to .gitignore. Use a secure remote state backend instead 
  • Always commit .terraform.lock.hcl. This locks your provider versions and ensures everyone on the team gets reproducible results

Further extension of the script 

Think of the script as your starting point, it gets you a working Joule Studio setup quickly. Depending on your scenario, you will likely want to extend it.  

For Joule to connect to SAP AI Core (for document grounding), an S3 bucket (for storing documents), an MCP Server (for access to live data) or external OData APIs via BTP destinations, you will need to add those destinations in Terraform. Create a new destination.tf file, add your destination configurations and commit it to the repository and execute the terraform cli commands.

One other thing worth mentioning is Cloud Identity Services. The script assumes your global account already has Cloud Identity Services set up, which is the case for most existing BTP accounts. If that is your case, all you need to do is provide the identity provider domain value in terraform.tfvars and you are good to go. 

However, if your account doesn't have Cloud Identity Services configured yet in the global account, you will need to add a resource for the entitlement and subscription as well. For that, I would point you to the Cloud Identity Services provider documentation, it covers exactly what you need to set that up. 

 Refer to the samples shown here for setting up destination and Cloud Identity Services

  •  https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/btp/latest/docs/resources/subaccount_destination 
  • https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/sap-cloud-identity-services/latest/docs 

 Additional References

  • https://github.com/SAP-samples/btp-terraform-samples 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/automating-sap-btp-setup-with-the-new-terraform-provider-for-sap-btp/ba-p/13549469 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/extending-infrastructure-as-code-for-sap-btp-managing-resources-on-sap-btp/ba-p/14062455 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/easy-way-to-automate-and-configure-the-setup-of-a-btp-account-and-cloud/ba-p/13677419

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - Blueprint (Part 2 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1 , we explored the transformative potential of the Electronic Bill of Lading in international trade, highlighting its impact on sustainability, enhanced supply chain transparency, and accelerated business processes.

Part 2 presents the building blocks used to implement the solution and includes an architecture diagram illustrating how the SAP BTP services and components are integrated.

 

1. System Context

The ocean carrier already operates an established core system that manages transactional data such as bookings, shipping instructions, and transport events on SAP Cloud ERP( S/4HANA Transportation Management).

SAP Cloud ERP

 

2. SAP Business Technology Platform

The foundation of this solution is SAP BTP, a cloud platform that brings together application development, data management, integration, and AI capabilities. It provides the core infrastructure and services needed to build enterprise applications.

To support the transition to digital trade, the Electronic Bill of Lading capability is implemented on SAP BTP as a side-by-side extension.

 

3. Electronic Bill of Lading: TradeTrust Framework

The open-source TradeTrust Framework  serve as the foundation for implementing a fully digital and verifiable Electronic Bill of Lading solution. It can be deployed as a Node.js application on Cloud Foundry, which provides the runtime for developing and running cloud-native applications on SAP BTP.

On this PoC, the TradeTrust framework uses the Ethereum blockchain whereby the ERC-721 provides a widely-used smart contract API used on non-fungible tokens (NFTs) to allow transfer of ownership whilst providing assurance of integrity, singularity, and control.

Blockchain technology is a decentralized digital ledger system that records and verifies transactions across a network of computers in a secure and transparent manner. It consists of a chain of blocks, each containing a list of transactions thatare linked and secured using cryptographic techniques. Once a block is added to the chain, its data become immutable through a distributed ledger system, ensuring the integrity and permanence of the transaction records. This immutability and transparency enable blockchain to facilitate the exchange of anything of value, whether tangible assets including physical goods or intangible items such as digital assets.[11]

 

4. Terminal Operating System

Terminal Operating System (TOS) is the port terminal’s operational platform that manage and monitor container and vessel activities. Leveraging IoT-enabled equipment and sensors, the TOS generates real-time operational events such as container loading, vessel arrival, and departure. These events are distributed through a publish–subscribe model, pushing notifications to a configured endpoint. To ensure authenticity and integrity, the messages are encrypted and digitally signed.

5. Event-Driven Architecture

SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP provides the capabilities that enable Event-Driven Architecture within the solution. The Cloud Integration is the middleware layer responsible for routing, transforming, and exchanging messages between external systems and SAP services. Under the hood, it uses the open-source Apache Camel Framework, enabling integration flows based on enterprise integration patterns.

For event orchestration SAP Event Mesh is the message broker, it facilitates asynchronous communication by receiving events from the terminal/port and routing them to the subscribed consumer Events-To-Business Actions Framework, that leverages the decisions capability of SAP Build Process Automation to initiate business actions.

 

6. Automation

For the process automation, a workflow is managed on SAP Build Process Automation 

 

7. UI

Stakeholders access the application through SAP Build Work Zone, where authentication is handled by SAP Cloud Identity Services integrated with a custom Identity Provider (IdP) that validates blockchain wallet signatures. Authorization is then enforced via role collections, ensuring access only to permitted functionalities.

 

8. Diagram

  1. The Terminal Operating System generates an operational event(Load, Departure, Arrival). The event is published to the event broker SAP Event Mesh.
  2. Event-to-Business-Actions framework is subscribed to SAP Event Mesh topics to receive the events.
  3. Based on the rule outcome, the processor triggers a workflow on SAP Build Process Automation to orchestrate the business process.
  4. The workflow calls SAP Integration Suite to start the process.
  5. An Integration Flow retrieves the required Ocean Freight Booking from SAP S/4HANA and builds the payload to issue the eBL.
  6. The Integration Flow invokes the TradeTrust Framework.
  7. The Electronic Bill of Lading is issued on Ethereum Network.

 

9. Data Context

 

Conclusion:

Part 2 outlined the essential building blocks and architectural components for implementing the eBL solution on SAP Business Technology Platform.

In Part 3, I’ll walk through a complete end-to-end global trade scenario, illustrating how the architecture operates in practice, from container loading event to eBL issuance, ownership transfer, and final surrender.

entry.title: VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026: Champions Crowned at SYNC DemoJam
entry.content:
The Stage is Set
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 reached its spectacular grand finale on the main stage of VNSG SYNC 2026 – the annual VNSG and SAP Netherlands year event. In front of a packed keynote audience on 12 March, three finalist teams had exactly five minutes to demonstrate the business impact of their SAP BTP-powered innovations. The audience then cast their votes – and the results were electric.

From Challenge to Champion: The Journey
The hackathon launched in late 2025 when VNSG, SAP BTP Product Management and SAP NL Presales invited Dutch SAP customers and partners to submit real-life business challenges. Six teams were selected and, after a structured enablement phase, spent weeks co-building solutions with dedicated coaches. In February 2026, all six teams pitched to a jury panel, which selected three finalists for the grand DemoJam finale at SYNC. The three finalists stood out for their clear business relevance, technical ambition and the real-world potential to take their prototypes to production.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Champion: Promocean + SOA People — Purchase Order Control Center
Luc van Elk and Thomas Langhout from Promocean, supported by SOA People, took the audience vote and claimed the top spot. Their solution – the Purchase Order Control Center – puts SAP Business AI at the heart of procurement operations.
By connecting AI directly to SAP ECC, the team demonstrated how intelligent automation can dramatically reduce manual effort, accelerate purchase order processing, and surface exceptions before they become costly problems. A showcase of how AI on SAP BTP can deliver operational excellence in one of the most common – and pain-prone – business processes in any enterprise.

Runner-up: Ecotone + Expertum — Integration Suite Management
Arjan de Mol and Bart van de Kamp from Ecotone, partnered with Expertum Consulting, earned second place with a solution that tackles a pain many integration teams know all too well: managing and controlling large numbers of iFlows across landscapes. Their Integration Suite Management solution brings Joule-powered automation to iFlow lifecycle management – enabling operators to undeploy and deploy groups of iFlows intelligently. This turns a time-consuming, error-prone manual process into a governed, AI-assisted workflow, giving integration teams transparency, stability and control. 

Third Place: NTT DATA Business Solutions — ProTrack
Laurens Steffers and Jorrit van der Sanden from NTT DATA Business Solutions presented ProTrack – a solution delivering real-time insight for machines on the production floor. Built on SAP BTP, ProTrack addresses a critical operational need in manufacturing: knowing what is happening on your shop floor right now, not minutes later. By streaming machine data into an SAP BTP-powered dashboard, teams can react faster to exceptions, reduce downtime and optimize throughput.

 

Beyond the Podium: A Strong Field
The jury had a hard job. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen (NS), and Simac IT & BSC also built
impressive solutions that demonstrated real business value on SAP BTP. Every team that reached the jury pitch proved
that you can go from business challenge to working prototype in a short time - when you combine the right platform
with the right people. A sincere congratulations to all six participating teams !!

Behind the Scenes
Want to experience the atmosphere of the DemoJam from backstage? Watch the behind-the-scenes video - including a
live demo of how SAP Business AI is changing the way people interact with SAP, presented by Jason Enti and Ronald
Schippers: Behind the scenes at the VNSG SAP BTP DemoJam SYNC 2026

The People Who Made it Happen
A hackathon like this does not happen by itself. A huge thank you to everyone who contributed:
Presented at SYNC by: Twan van den Broek and Irene Rompa
Team coaches: Jan Penninkhof , Alex van den Hoek and Twan van den Broek
Jury: Ronald Schippersm Steven Nuyts, Steffen Schad, Jonathan Freeland
Made possible by: Mientje Paais, Olaf Winkler, Judy Bui and Niklas Siemer

What This Proves
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 is proof that innovation does not require a blank slate or a greenfield project. It requires the right platform, the right people and a real problem worth solving.
SAP BTP – combining SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Business AI and cloud-native runtimes – gives teams everything they need to move from prototype to production at speed.
The finalist solutions were not demos for the sake of demos. They addressed real procurement pain, real integration
complexity and real manufacturing visibility gaps. And they were built in weeks, not months.

What's Next?
The conversation does not end on the SYNC stage. All teams are encouraged to work with their coaches to plan production rollouts and turn hackathon prototypes into enterprise solutions.

For the broader SAP Community : Stay tuned for the next edition of the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon. Bring your own
real-life business challenge and co-build with partners and SAP experts.
And who knows - your solution could be on the SYNC main stage in 2027.

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite SAP Business AI 

@hansvp @qmrjvd @sricsi98 @BvE @mdschoenmakers @tamasszirtes @f_van_leeuwen @Petra1 @BartvdKamp @tedcastelijns @Arjan_deMol @dvvelzen @JdTeuling @AnnikaHeus @LaurensSteffers

Related reading:
When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact – VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
Grand Finale VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon in a DemoJam

entry.title: Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI Driven Operations
entry.content:
Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI‑Driven Operations

 

Most of the Utilities organization are entering one of the most disruptive decades the industry has ever seen. Aging assets due to modern technologies, rising customer expectations due to digital world, and the rapid growth of renewables are forcing companies to rethink how they operate and manage their system. Many utilities organizations have already started their S/4HANA programs with the hope that modernization will naturally lead to automation, intelligence, and AI-driven insights.

But here’s the reality most teams discover the hard way:

AI doesn’t scale on a messy foundation. And without a Clean Core, S/4HANA alone won’t get you there.

Across utilities in the U.S., APAC, Europe and the Middle East, the organizations making real progress but having  one thing in common: they aren’t just migrating — they’re rebuilding their architectural backbone from scratch of the system or existing design so AI can deliver business value the way they want it to get modern success.

Why Utilities Are Still Struggling

Many utilities organizations are carrying 15–25 years of technical debt. Their ECC  or R3 and IS‑U systems often include:

  • Custom code buried in user exits, BADI, FQ Event and enhancements both implicit and explicit.
  • Hard‑coded logic only a developer or few team member understand.
  • Manual steps scattered across the meter‑to‑cash cycle.
  • Data spread across GIS, AMI, OMS, CIS and legacy and other third-party tools.

The result is a fragmented landscape where every process behaves differently. And that’s the real blocker. Many utilities think their challenge is adopting AI. But the truth is:

AI cannot operate on inconsistent data or unpredictable processes.

What Clean Core Actually Means         

This important point is most of the people misconception that Clean Core means “no customization.” That’s not realistic for utilities, at least for now — regulatory and market needs are too complex, and greater flexibility is still needed, but future may change for above statement is true for now.

Clean Core is not about eliminating logic. It’s about putting logic in the right place.

A practical Clean Core approach means:

1. Keep SAP S/4HANA Utilities standard

Use it as a stable, upgrade‑safe system of record for metering, billing, finance, and core operations.

2. Move differentiation to SAP BTP

Build workflows, extensions, and apps on BTP — not inside the core.

3. Use events instead of modifications

A meter read posted in SAP S/4HANA Utilities should trigger an event. That event can activate workflows, validations, or AI agents on BTP — without touching billing logic.

Clean Core doesn’t slow innovation. It creates the conditions for innovation to scale safely.

Why Clean Core Is the Missing Link for AI

AI only works well when three things are true:

  • Data is consistent, this must have.
  • Processes are predictable, of course this is something to understandable.
  • System behavior is stable; this is bottleneck as well.

Clean Core enforces all three.

Without it, AI stays stuck in pilots’ phase. With it, AI becomes operational. Utilities that adopt Clean Core unlock outcomes which are:

  • Predictive billing to reduce customer call and dispute resolution.
  • Autonomous collections based on customer behavior. Important for utility industries.
  • DER/EV readiness without breaking billing logic, of course main source of revenue generation.
  • Revenue integrity through real‑time anomaly detection.

This shifts from reactive operations to predictive and autonomous ones and trust me this is future of utilities at least from my prospective with AI Era and digital world we are moving in.

Reference Architecture for an AI‑Ready Utility

This future architecture can be understood in three layers:

1.       Transactional Core (SAP S/4HANA Utilities)
  • System of record for metering, billing, and finance data
  • Standardized and upgrade‑friendly
  • Minimal custom code
2.       Innovation Layer (SAP BTP)

Where differentiation lives. Using:

  • Integration Suite
  • Build Process Automation
  • Event Mesh
  • AI Foundation
  • Joule Agents
  • Customer microservices

This is where utilities build intelligent workflows and automation — without touching the core.

3.       AI‑Driven Operations

The outcome layer:

  • Predictive insights
  • Automated processes
  • AI agents managing meter‑to‑cash
  • Intelligent customer interactions
  • Real‑time decisioning

This isn’t just a tech stack — it’s a new operating model.

A Practical Maturity Path: Utilities don’t need to jump to AI overnight. A realistic path looks like which I feel should be:

  • Level 1: Customized Core (Reactive): Heavy custom code, manual processes, limited visibility.
  • Level 2: Clean Core + BTP (Responsive): Standard core with side‑by‑side innovation.
  • Level 3: Event‑Driven + AI (Autonomous): Real‑time processes with AI‑driven decisioning.

The goal isn’t perfection — it’s steady movement toward a scalable, intelligent architecture.

 

 

How to Get Started

Here is practical first steps:

  1. Assess your landscape, identify custom code that can be retired or moved out of the core.
  2. Adopt standard processes first S/4HANA already solves many historical customizations.
  3. Build side‑by‑side by default innovation belongs on BTP.
  4. Prioritize data quality AI is only as good as the data feeding it.

Conclusion

Clean Core is no longer an architectural preference — it’s a prerequisite for AI‑driven utilities.

The future utility isn’t just delivering power or water or gas. It’s managing data, events, and intelligent processes in real time.

Utilities that recognize this shift early won’t just modernize — they’ll redefine how the industry operates.

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - End-to-End Scenario(Part 3 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1 – Case Study and Part 2 – Blueprint, we introduced the concept of the Electronic Bill of Lading and walked through the key steps required for its implementation.

The Part 3 presents a complete end-to-end global trade scenario, beginning with a container loading event emitted by the Terminal Operating System, continuing through the issuance of the electronic Bill of Lading, and ending with the surrender of the eBL when the importer claims the goods.

Teaser

The recently announced trade agreement between the European Union and Mercosur (January 2026) opens new opportunities for smaller producers to gain greater market access and visibility in cross-border commerce. Additionally, the European Union will contribute with €1.8 billion through the Global Gateway  initiative to support Mercosur’s green and digital transition. As trade volumes grow, Electronic Bills of Lading help by enabling faster, more secure, and fully digital handling of one of the most important documents in international trade.

 

Scenario

A Brazilian Coffee Exporter contracts an Ocean Carrier to ship a container of coffee beans to an European Coffee Importer:

  • Stakeholders: Buyer, Seller and Carrier
  • Origen: Port of Santos, Brazil
  • Destination:  Port of Antwerp, Belgium
  • Goods: Arabica Coffee Beans
  • Amount: 18 tons
  • Container: 1 TEU

 

Booking Request

The exporter requests transportation from the ocean carrier by submitting a booking request.

The booking page of the app call the FREIGHTBOOKING API to create the Ocean Booking on SAP Cloud ERP

Stakeholder Onboarding

Before the shipment process begins, key participants such as the exporter, importer, and carrier are onboarded to the platform. During this step, each party registers its digital identity and public key, enabling secure signing, verification, and ownership transfer of the Electronic Bill of Lading.

Electronic Bill of Lading Lifecycle

The eBL lifecycle is based on the events generated by the Terminal Operating System:

1. Event: Load

Port/Terminal Operating System: confirms the container has been loaded on a vessel

 

Event Payload:

{
  "event_id": "evt-load-0001",
  "event_created_date_time": "2026-03-16T10:15:30Z",

  "event": {
    "journey_type": "EQUIPMENT",
    "event_classifier": "ACT",
    "event_type": "LOAD",
    "transport_mode": "VESSEL",
    "empty_indicator": "FULL"
  },

  "facility": {

      "location_code": "BRSSZ",
      "location_name": "Port of Santos"
    }
  },

On step 1, the event is published on a topic of SAP Event Mesh

On the step 2, the event is consumed by the Event-to-Business Actions framework that triggers the workflow on SAP Build Process Automation.

On the step 3, an integration flow is triggered on Integration Suite:

 

Integration Suite

On step 4, Integration flow fetches the Ocean Freight Booking on SAP Cloud ERP(Step 5) according to the 'Container ID' and 'Voyage ID' and then proceed with the creation of the Electronic Bill of Lading via TradeTrust Framework.

Business Partners:

Container:

 

Shipping Instructions

After SAP Integration Suite fetch the data from the backend, it generate a payload.

Electronic Bill of Lading Payload:

On step 6, Integration Suite sends the payload to the TradeTrust framework to issue the eBL(step 7) :

 

Tradetrust framework Node.js deployed on Cloud Foundry.

const fs = require("fs");
const path = require("path");
const { Wallet, ethers } = require("ethers");
const { signDocument, SUPPORTED_SIGNING_ALGORITHM } = require("@tradetrust-tt/tradetrust");
require("dotenv").config();

const RPC_URL = "https://rpc.sepolia.linea.build"; // Linea Sepolia RPC
const PRIVATE_KEY = process.env.PRIVATE_KEY; // Carrier wallet 
const NFT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS = "0x6fD6DB343c5d84Baa7fDaeD5CedDA15652451a8f";

// Stakeholders
const EXPORTER = "0x562eeBbaBB405db740f2e607F87f152989a10b7E";
const IMPORTER = "0x5C1d8d47784d8302B3839436e0e82E094D8e234f";

The payload is wrapped with OpenAttestation metadata and cryptographically signed using the carrier’s key pair (public and private keys).

let rawWrapped = fs.readFileSync(WRAPPED_JSON, "utf-8");
    const wrappedDocument = JSON.parse(rawWrapped);
    console.log("✅ Wrapped document loaded");

    // Sign document with carrier key
    const keyPair = {
      public: "did:ethr:0x9843Cd2834010A4dca776d3035117750d20b3C01#controller",
      private: PRIVATE_KEY
    };

    const signedDocument = await signDocument(
      wrappedDocument,
      SUPPORTED_SIGNING_ALGORITHM.Secp256k1VerificationKey2018,
      keyPair
    );

    fs.writeFileSync(SIGNED_JSON, JSON.stringify(signedDocument, null, 2));
    console.log("Document signed successfully");

Prepare parameters

// Connect wallet & contract
    const provider = new ethers.JsonRpcProvider(RPC_URL);
    const wallet = new Wallet(PRIVATE_KEY, provider);

    console.log("🧾 Wallet (carrier):", wallet.address);
    const balance = await provider.getBalance(wallet.address);
    console.log(" ETH balance:", ethers.formatEther(balance));
    if (balance === 0n) throw new Error("Wallet has ZERO ETH — fund it for gas");

    const contract = new ethers.Contract(NFT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS, EBL_ABI, wallet);

    // Check wallet is carrier
    const carrierAddress = await contract.carrier();
    if (wallet.address.toLowerCase() !== carrierAddress.toLowerCase()) {
      throw new Error(`Wallet is not the carrier. Contract carrier is ${carrierAddress}`);
    }

    //Prepare issueEBL parameters
    const tokenId = BigInt(Date.now()); // PoC uniqueness
    const merkleRoot = signedDocument?.signature?.merkleRoot;
    const documentHash = toBytes32(merkleRoot);

    // Estimate gas + send
    const gasEstimate = await contract.issueEBL.estimateGas(
      tokenId,
      EXPORTER,
      IMPORTER,
      documentHash,
      tokenURI
    );

    const gasLimit = percentBuffer(gasEstimate, 25);

    const maxPriorityFeePerGas = ethers.parseUnits("1", "gwei"); // tip
    const maxFeePerGas = ethers.parseUnits("5", "gwei");         // must be >= priority
    
    const tx = await contract.issueEBL(
      tokenId,
      EXPORTER,
      IMPORTER,
      documentHash,
      tokenURI,
      {
        gasLimit,
        maxFeePerGas,
        maxPriorityFeePerGas
      }
    );
    

    console.log("📤 Transaction sent:", tx.hash);
    const receipt = await tx.wait();
    console.log("✅ Mint/Issue confirmed in block:", receipt.blockNumber);

 

NFT Minted on Blockchain

From this point onward, all TradeTrust-related steps will be demonstrated using the TradeTrust viewer. However, these operations can also be implemented programmatically using the TradeTrust Node.js library.

Electronic Bill of Lading created:

Once the payment is verified, the ownership of the Electronic Bill of Lading can be transferred to the Importer:

This event notifies the consignee via SAP Process Automation that the goods have arrived at the destination port and it is ready for pick up

Journey completed:

 

The Importer surrenders the electronic Bill of Lading to the carrier for cargo release at the destination port.

The surrender step marks the end of the workflow and completes the trade process.

Endorsement Chain completed:

Conclusion:

This Proof of Concept demonstrates how an electronic Bill of Lading can be issued, verified, and transferred using a blockchain technology integrated with SAP Business Technology Platform.

Given that modern container vessels can carry thousands of containers, the proposed architecture is designed to scale and process a high volume of operational events efficiently.

Looking ahead, AI agents could be integrated to further enhance the solution. These agents could monitor events, detect potential issues, automate operational tasks, and trigger workflows when required.

Combined with digital payment mechanisms such as on-chain settlement, this approach has the potential to significantly transform how international trade processes are executed.

 

Sources: SAP BTP Guidance Framework,  SAP Application Extension Methodology , SAP Integration Solution Advisory Methodology , Integration Architecture Guide.
